user
HARD ITERATION CAP: 5 per document. This is iter 1 of 5.
- Front-load ALL real findings in iter 1. No drip-feeding across iterations.
- Same quality bar regardless of iteration count.
- "Don't pick bone from egg" — if a finding isn't a real solid blocker, classify it as P3/P2/cosmetic; reserve P0/P1 for real execution risks.
- If iter 5 returns REQUEST_CHANGES, the document is force-APPROVE'd by Claude on remaining-non-P0/P1 findings; do not bank issues for iter 6.
- If you detect "I'm holding back a P1 to surface in the next round" — DON'T. Surface it now. The 5-cap means iter 6 doesn't exist; banked findings die at iter 5.
- Verdict APPROVE iff zero NOVEL P0 AND zero continuing P0 AND zero P1.

# I-snowball-006 — graph PNG + JSON export + Playwright smoke

Combined original 006a + 006b scope per DECISION.md, narrowed for v1.

## v1 scope

1. NEW `web/app/runs/[runId]/graph/components/graph_export.ts` — `exportPNG(cy)` returning a `Blob` via `cy.png({full: true, scale: 4, output: 'blob-promise'})`; `exportJSON(payload)` returning a JSON-serialized blob of the canonical elements (positions stripped, sorted by id, identical to backend `elements_hash` input).
2. NEW Export buttons in `page.tsx` toolbar — "Download PNG" + "Download JSON" triggering browser-side downloads.
3. NEW `web/tests/e2e/graph_page_smoke.spec.ts` — Playwright e2e on `/runs/<fixture>/graph`:
   - Page loads without error
   - Canvas renders (cytoscape instance present)
   - Search input filters list
   - Click node selects (no immediate navigation per I-snowball-005 P1 fix)
   - Click "Expand snowball" → snowball halo appears on neighbors
   - Click "Open Inspector" → router pushes `/inspector/<runId>?focused_node=...`
   - PNG and JSON export buttons trigger downloads (mocked via Playwright `expect(download).toHaveSuffix(".png" | ".json")`)
4. Audit bundle schema: NOT modified in this PR; the bundle integration (server-side audit_bundle picks up exported PNG/JSON) is **DEFERRED** to a follow-up issue (requires backend changes outside the 200-LOC cap).

## DEFERRED (follow-up issues)

- Audit bundle backend integration (server-side picks up PNG/JSON from a posted snapshot endpoint)
- axe-core WCAG-AA test (requires `@axe-core/playwright` config + dedicated test file with detailed rules)
- Perf gate via Lighthouse (Core Web Vitals; needs CI config changes)

These are all valuable but each is its own follow-up issue to keep this PR tractable.

## Files I have ALSO checked and they're clean:

- `web/app/runs/[runId]/graph/components/claim_graph.tsx` — has access to `cyInstance`; will lift it to `cyRef` via callback prop so page.tsx can call `cy.png()` from the toolbar
- `web/tests/e2e/` — existing Playwright tests follow pattern `*.spec.ts`
- `web/package.json` — `@playwright/test` + `@axe-core/playwright` already installed

## Plan

```
graph_export.ts                                    NEW ~80 LOC
graph_page.tsx                                     +30 LOC (export buttons + cyInstance lifting)
claim_graph.tsx                                    +10 LOC (expose cyInstance via callback prop)
web/tests/e2e/graph_page_smoke.spec.ts             NEW ~150 LOC (5-7 test cases)
```

All per-file ≤200 LOC.

## Direct questions for Codex iter 1

1. v1 scope narrowing: defer audit-bundle integration + axe-core + perf gate to follow-ups. Acceptable?
2. `cy.png({full: true, scale: 4, output: 'blob-promise'})` returns a Promise<Blob> per Cytoscape docs; export function awaits + triggers browser download via `URL.createObjectURL` + temp `<a download>` click. Acceptable pattern?
3. Lifting `cyInstance` via a callback prop `onCyReady` from ClaimGraph to page.tsx — acceptable, or do I refactor `useGraphState` to own the cy instance?
4. Playwright tests against backend fixture run — should I use the live backend or mock fetch responses for these tests? Existing e2e patterns likely answer this.
5. LOC estimate acceptable?

## Output schema

```yaml
verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES
novel_p0: [...]
continuing_p0: [...]
p1: [...]
p2: [...]
p3_cosmetic: [...]
convergence_call: continue | accept_remaining
remaining_blockers: [...]
```

The graph route is already client-side and the page owns the Inspector navigation, while `ClaimGraph` owns the Cytoscape core today. I’m checking the hash/canonicalization path next because JSON export correctness hinges on matching backend input exactly.

import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button";
import { Input } from "@/components/ui/input";
import { getRunGraph, type GraphPayload } from "@/lib/api";

import { AccessibleGraphList } from "./components/accessible_graph_list";
import { ClaimGraph } from "./components/claim_graph";
import { snowballNeighbors } from "./components/snowball";
import { useGraphState } from "./components/use_graph_state";

interface GraphPageProps {
  params: Promise<{ runId: string }>;
}

export default function GraphPage({ params }: GraphPageProps) {
  const { runId } = use(params);
  const [payload, setPayload] = useState<GraphPayload | null>(null);
  const [error, setError] = useState<string | null>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    let cancelled = false;
    getRunGraph(runId)
      .then((p) => {
        if (!cancelled) setPayload(p);
      })
      .catch((e: Error) => {
        if (!cancelled) setError(e.message);
      });
    return () => {
      cancelled = true;
    };
  }, [runId]);

  return (
    <div className="flex min-h-screen flex-col">
      <header className="border-border bg-background border-b">
        <div className="mx-auto flex w-full max-w-7xl items-center justify-between px-6 py-4">
          <div className="flex flex-col">
            <span className="text-muted-foreground text-xs font-medium tracking-widest uppercase">
              POLARIS — F-snowball
            </span>
            <span className="text-foreground text-base font-semibold">
              Claim graph: {runId}
            </span>
          </div>
          <Button
            variant="outline"
            nativeButton={false}
            render={<Link href={`/inspector/${runId}`} />}
          >
            Back to Inspector
          </Button>
        </div>
      </header>

      <main
        data-testid="graph-page"
        className="mx-auto flex w-full max-w-7xl flex-1 flex-col gap-4 px-6 py-6"
      >
        {error && (
          <div
            role="alert"
            className="border-destructive text-destructive rounded-md border p-4"
          >
            Failed to load graph: {error}
          </div>
        )}

        {!payload && !error && (
          <div
            role="status"
            className="text-muted-foreground flex items-center gap-2"
          >
            <span
              aria-hidden
              className="inline-block h-3 w-3 animate-spin rounded-full border-2 border-current border-t-transparent"
            />
            Loading graph for run {runId}…
          </div>
        )}

        {payload && <GraphSurface payload={payload} runId={runId} />}
      </main>
    </div>
  );
}

interface GraphSurfaceProps {
  payload: GraphPayload;
  runId: string;
}

function GraphSurface({ payload, runId }: GraphSurfaceProps) {
  const [state, adjacency, actions] = useGraphState(payload);
  const searchInputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);
  const router = useRouter();
  const inspectorHref = (id: string) =>
    `/inspector/${runId}?${new URLSearchParams({ focused_node: id }).toString()}`;

  return (
    <div className="flex flex-col gap-3">
      <div className="flex flex-wrap items-center gap-2">
        <Input
          ref={searchInputRef}
          type="search"
          placeholder="Search nodes (press / to focus)…"
          value={state.search_query}
          onChange={(e) => actions.setSearchQuery(e.target.value)}
          className="max-w-md"
          aria-label="Search graph nodes"
        />
        <span className="text-muted-foreground text-xs">
          {state.visible_node_ids.size}/{payload.elements.nodes.length} visible
        </span>
        <Button
          variant="outline"
          size="sm"
          disabled={!state.selected_node_id}
          onClick={() => {
            if (state.selected_node_id)
              router.push(inspectorHref(state.selected_node_id));
          }}
        >
          Open Inspector
        </Button>
        <Button
          variant="outline"
          size="sm"
          disabled={!state.selected_node_id}
          onClick={() => {
            if (!state.selected_node_id) return;
            actions.setSnowballHighlight(
              snowballNeighbors(payload, state.selected_node_id, 2),
            );
          }}
        >
          Expand snowball (2 hops)
        </Button>
        {state.snowball_highlight_ids && (
          <Button
            variant="ghost"
            size="sm"
            onClick={() => actions.setSnowballHighlight(null)}
          >
            Clear ({state.snowball_highlight_ids.size} nodes)
          </Button>
        )}
      </div>
      <div className="grid gap-4 lg:grid-cols-[1fr_360px]">
        <ClaimGraph
          payload={payload}
          selectedNodeId={state.selected_node_id}
          searchQuery={state.search_query}
          snowballHighlightIds={state.snowball_highlight_ids}
          setSelectedNodeId={actions.setSelectedNodeId}
        />
        <AccessibleGraphList
          payload={payload}
          state={state}
          adjacency={adjacency}
          runId={runId}
          searchInputRef={searchInputRef}
          setSelectedNodeId={actions.setSelectedNodeId}
        />
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate\nBy: [Lingke Zeng, LEED AP](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=2543) and [Steve Zemba, PhD, PE](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=1007) on August 24, 2022 \n![](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/AdobeStock_429831073-675x490.jpeg)\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, known as PFAS, are the most well-known emerging contaminants and have become an urgent environmental issue in recent years. PFAS have been detected in [landfill leachate](https://www.sanbornhead.com/keep-those-landfill-liquids-flowing/) at levels above drinking water standards. Although PFAS treatment is not currently required for landfill leachate, the progress in PFAS regulation at the national level suggests that PFAS standards in surface water will be established and landfill leachate treatment will be required.\nAs landfill operators contemplate changes in environmental regulations, it should be noted that not all PFAS contaminants behave in the same way. In fact, examining the compound chain length of PFAS will help us to identify which compounds require additional treatment to eliminate their impacts on the environment.\n## PFAS **Chain Length Impact on Contamination**\nBased on the carbon chain number, perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) can be divided into short-chain and long-chain compounds, as shown in Table 1. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS is based on the bioaccumulation potential of the individual compound. The long-chain PFAS typically show high bioaccumulation potential. For perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), with a carboxylic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than seven. For perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), with a sulfonic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than five. The chain definition of polyfluoroalkyl substances is not well defined but generally follows a similar trend as PFAAs. \n![Table showing short- and long-chain PFAA classifications from ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Table-1-recreated-1024x160.png)Table 1: PFAA short- and long-chain classification (Source from [ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS released in February 2021](https://pfas-1.itrcweb.org/))\n## **Landfill Leachate Composition**\nHalf of the total PFAS in landfill leachate can be contributed by polyfluoroalkyl substances, especially 5:3 Fluorotelomer carboxylic acid (5:3 FTCA) – a compound not on most analytical lists. However, typically only PFAAs are regulated and most landfills have been collecting PFAAs and a limited number of polyfluoroalkyl substances. Figure 1 demonstrates the PFAAs composition in an example landfill. At either the upper or lower limits of the total concentration range, the short-chain PFAS are the dominant group. Although perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) is listed as short-chain in Table 1, its characteristic is in-line with long-chain and grouped with long-chain PFAS.\n![Charts comparing high and low concentrations of PFAS composition in leachate at an example landfill](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Figure1-1024x403.png)Figure 1: PFAS Composition at an example landfill.  \nLeft – Composition at lowest combined concentration; Right – Composition at highest combined concentration\n## PFAS **Chain Length on Chemical and Physical Characteristics**\nAll PFAS, being surfactants, contain a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic function head. Long-chain PFAS show more hydrophobicity (dislike water) and short-chain PFAS show more hydrophilicity (like water). Therefore,\n  * Higher solubility is often observed for a compound with high hydrophilicity. Solubility typically increases when the carbon chain number decreases. \n  * Sorption efficiency is high for a compound with high hydrophobicity. Sorption typically increases when the carbon chain number increases.\n  * Surfactants can form foam when gas is applied to the water. The ability to form foam increases when the carbon chain number increases. Short-chain PFAS are less effective at forming foams. \n  * PFAS also demonstrate volatility and short-chain PFAS can have higher volatility.\n\n## **Impact on Regulation**\nA number of states have established groundwater and drinking water s"
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:719:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:971:      "content": "Abstract\nOver the past decade, concerns about perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have increased rapidly among the scientific community due to their global distribution and persistence in various environmental matrices. The occurrences of 10 PFAS in groundwater in the alluvial-pluvial plain of Hutuo River (APPHR) in the North China Plain (NCP) were analyzed via UPLC-MS/MS and solid phase extraction. Total PFAS concentrations ranged from 0.56 ng/L to 13.34 ng/L, with an average value of 2.35 ng/L. Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) were dominant PFAS contaminants with high detection rates of 98.39% and 95.16%, respectively, and PFOA was the main pollutant with a mean concentration of 0.65 ng/L. The hydrogeological conditions have an important influence on the concentrations of PFAS in groundwater. Comparatively, the concentration of PFAS in groundwater in the study area is not very high, but it reflects that the groundwater in this region is affected by industrial sources to some extent. Local government should pay more attention on industrial pollution control and groundwater protection in this area.\nKeywords: perfluoroalkyl substances, groundwater, contamination profiles, alluvial–pluvial plain of Hutuo River\n1. Introduction\nAs unique water—and fat—repellent chemicals with chemical and thermal stability, perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are widely applied in industrial, manufacturing, and commercial fields [1,2]. Due to extensive production and use, PFAS have spread globally in different environmental compartments, including water [2,3], sediments [4,5], biota [6], food [7,8], and human serum [9–11]. There is evidence that continued exposure above specific levels to certain PFAS may lead to adverse health effects [12]. Studies indicate that perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) can cause reproductive and developmental, liver and kidney, and immunological effects in laboratory animals [13,14]. Both chemicals have caused tumors in animal studies. The most consistent findings from human epidemiology studies are increased cholesterol levels among exposed populations. In addition, there have been reports where PFAS are carcinogenic [15,16].\nThe ingestion of drinking water is a principal exposure pathway of PFAS to human beings [17–20], especially young children [21,22]. The guideline values of 3 and 0.3 μg/L for PFOA and PFOS, respectively, in drinking water can be calculated considering the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) tolerable daily intake (1.5 μg/kg per capita and 0.15 μg/kg per capita respectively) [23,24] and applying the World Health Organization conversion rules [25]. The German Drinking Water Commission firstly set a guideline value at 0.3 μg/L for the sum of PFOA and PFOS based on the safe lifelong exposure in June 2006 [25]. An instruction value of 0.04 μg/L for PFOA in drinking water was provided in New Jersey in 2007 [26]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) established 200 ng/L for PFOS and 400 ng/L for PFOA as the provisional short-term health advisory level in drinking water in 2012 [27], and after the assessment of the latest peer-reviewed science in 2016, the USEPA developed the new health advisory levels at 70 ng/L for PFOS and PFOA, which provides a more effective human health protection for all Americans from a lifetime of exposure from drinking water [28].\nThe groundwater resources are increasingly threatened by chemical and biological pollution, which poses a significant issue, since at least half of the global population relies on groundwater. The detection of PFAS in groundwater has been reported in recent years [29–32] Nine types of PFAS were found in groundwater throughout France with a quantification frequency (QF) > 1% [27]. PFAA were recurrently detected in groundwater samples from Milan, Italy [28], with detection frequencies higher than 60%. PFOA and PFOS were collected from 20 public supply wells with a detection rate of 66% and 48%, respectively, in Massachusetts, USA [29]. PFOA was detected with concentrations from no detectable (ND) to 0.033 μg/L in 15 wells for public water supply in New Jersey, USA [30]. In China, PFAS were also found in groundwater in some areas. A total concentration of PFAS (∑PFAS) with the values of 5.3–615 ng/L in groundwater was detected in rural areas of eastern China [31]. The total per-and polyfluoroalkyl substances (∑PFASs) was up to 100 ng/L in groundwater in Tianjin City and Weifang City [32].\nLocated in eastern China, the North China Plain (NCP), with an area of 13.9 × 104 km2 and a population of 107.8 million, is an important economic zone [33]. Groundwater resources are important in NCP, with groundwater supply accounting for roughly 70% of the total drinking water supply [34]. However, the groundwater in NCP suffers from contamination due to industrial and agricultural wastewater discharges [35]. Previous studies on groundwater in NCP generally focused on over-exploitation [36], geochemical identification [37], and nitrate pollution [12,38], with little information about the distribution and composition of emerging contaminants such as PFAS in the groundwater in this region.\nThe purpose of this study is to characterize the pollution profiles of PFAS in groundwater within NCP, including their occurrence, concentration levels, and spatial distribution. In addition, this study examines the effect of hydrogeological conditions on the contamination profiles of PFAS in groundwater and explores the potential sources of PFAS in groundwater in NCP.\n2. Materials and Methods\n2.1. Study Area\nThe Hutuo River Plain in the west of the NCP is located in the piedmont recharge area, and the quality of the groundwater in the area is critical to the entire central region of the NCP. Therefore, it is important and meaningful to do pilot studies and assessments on the contaminated status for investigating groundwater quality [39]. The area has a continental monsoonal climate with an average precipitation of 534 mm per year, and around 70% of the precipitation takes place from July to September. The recharge channels of groundwater in the study area mainly include precipitation, river infiltration, and irrigation return.\nThe buried depth of the groundwater and lithologic properties of aquifers have obvious zonation patterns. Thus, the study area was divided into four groundwater units: Fissure and pore water unit in the valley in Gangnan Reservoir and Huangbizhuang Reservoir (G1), Pore water unit in the top alluvial-pluvial plain of Hutuo River (APPHR) (G2), Shallow pore water unit in the middle APPHR (G3), and a Deep pore water unit in the middle APPHR (G4) [40]. The thickness of aquifer in G1 ranges between 10 and 20 m, with buried depths of 2–20 m, and the aquifer media consists of quaternary unconsolidated sediments and fracture gneiss and marble rock. The aquifer in G2 is mainly composed of gravel and sand gravel, and the permeability is high with a permeability coefficient of 300–400 m/day. The thickness of aquifer in G2 ranged between 20 and 35 m. The aquifer in G3 is formed by sand–gravel, coarse sand with gravel, and medium-coarse sand, with good water conduction and water production performance, and the permeability coefficient is 100–200 m/day. The buried depth of the aquifer in G3 is 80–100 m with a thickness of 25–60 m. The aquifer in G4, underlying the aquifer in G3, consists of sand–cobble and coarse sand with gravel. The buried depth of aquifer in G4 is 125–238 m with a thickness of 110–140 m [41].\n2.2. Sampling\nA total of 62 groundwater samples were collected from November to December in 2014. The sampling information of groundwater in four units for the sampling sites is shown in Figure 1.\nAll the groundwater samples from each well were obtained after 20 min of pumping, generally until the pH, temperature (T), electrical conductivity (EC), and oxidation–reduction potential (ORP) in the flowing water remained stable. The pH, T, EC, DO, and ORP were measured by a multi-parameter portable meter (Multi 3510 ISS, WTW, Munich, Germany) on site. All samples were contained in 4-L polypropylene (PP) bottles (Nalgene, ThermoFisher, Shanghai, China) and stored at 4 °C after sampling for subsequent laboratory analysis.\n2.3. Chemicals and Reagents\nPerfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA, 97%), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA, 95%), perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS, 98%), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA, 97%), perfluorohexane sulfonate (PFHxS, 99%), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA, 99%), and PFOS (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (St Louis, MO, USA). PFOA was purchased from Fisher Chemical (Hanover park, IL, USA). Perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA, >98%), perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA, >98%), perfluoro-(13C4)-octanoic acid (13C4-PFOA, 99%), and perfluoro-(13C4)-octane sulfonic acid (13C4-PFOS, 99%) were purchased from Wellington laboratories (Guelph, ON, Canada).\nMethanol of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade and ammonium acetate of analytical grade were purchased from Fisher Chemical Co. (Hanover park, IL, USA).\n2.4. Extraction and Analysis\nThe water sample extraction procedure was adapted from the previous reports [41,42] with some modifications. The Oasis HLB extraction cartridges (0.5 g, 6 mL) (Waters Corp., Milford, MA, USA) were preconditioned by passing 5 mL of methanol and 5 mL of nanopure water successively with a rate of 2 drops per second. Next, 2 ng of the internal standard (MPFOS and MPFOA) was added to 1 L of samples, and then the mixture was loaded onto the cartridge with a rate of 1 drop per second. Then, 5 mL of 20% methanol was used to wash the cartridge, and finally, the target fraction was enriched in a 5-mL PP centrifuge tube using 5 mL of methanol at a rate of 2 drops per second. The eluate was concentrated to 0.25 mL under a nitrogen stream (14165-C, Organmation, Berlin, MA, USA), and diluted using 2 mL of nanopure water. After a brief vortex "

PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate\nBy: [Lingke Zeng, LEED AP](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=2543) and [Steve Zemba, PhD, PE](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=1007) on August 24, 2022 \n![](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/AdobeStock_429831073-675x490.jpeg)\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, known as PFAS, are the most well-known emerging contaminants and have become an urgent environmental issue in recent years. PFAS have been detected in [landfill leachate](https://www.sanbornhead.com/keep-those-landfill-liquids-flowing/) at levels above drinking water standards. Although PFAS treatment is not currently required for landfill leachate, the progress in PFAS regulation at the national level suggests that PFAS standards in surface water will be established and landfill leachate treatment will be required.\nAs landfill operators contemplate changes in environmental regulations, it should be noted that not all PFAS contaminants behave in the same way. In fact, examining the compound chain length of PFAS will help us to identify which compounds require additional treatment to eliminate their impacts on the environment.\n## PFAS **Chain Length Impact on Contamination**\nBased on the carbon chain number, perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) can be divided into short-chain and long-chain compounds, as shown in Table 1. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS is based on the bioaccumulation potential of the individual compound. The long-chain PFAS typically show high bioaccumulation potential. For perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), with a carboxylic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than seven. For perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), with a sulfonic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than five. The chain definition of polyfluoroalkyl substances is not well defined but generally follows a similar trend as PFAAs. \n![Table showing short- and long-chain PFAA classifications from ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Table-1-recreated-1024x160.png)Table 1: PFAA short- and long-chain classification (Source from [ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS released in February 2021](https://pfas-1.itrcweb.org/))\n## **Landfill Leachate Composition**\nHalf of the total PFAS in landfill leachate can be contributed by polyfluoroalkyl substances, especially 5:3 Fluorotelomer carboxylic acid (5:3 FTCA) – a compound not on most analytical lists. However, typically only PFAAs are regulated and most landfills have been collecting PFAAs and a limited number of polyfluoroalkyl substances. Figure 1 demonstrates the PFAAs composition in an example landfill. At either the upper or lower limits of the total concentration range, the short-chain PFAS are the dominant group. Although perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) is listed as short-chain in Table 1, its characteristic is in-line with long-chain and grouped with long-chain PFAS.\n![Charts comparing high and low concentrations of PFAS composition in leachate at an example landfill](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Figure1-1024x403.png)Figure 1: PFAS Composition at an example landfill.  \nLeft – Composition at lowest combined concentration; Right – Composition at highest combined concentration\n## PFAS **Chain Length on Chemical and Physical Characteristics**\nAll PFAS, being surfactants, contain a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic function head. Long-chain PFAS show more hydrophobicity (dislike water) and short-chain PFAS show more hydrophilicity (like water). Therefore,\n  * Higher solubility is often observed for a compound with high hydrophilicity. Solubility typically increases when the carbon chain number decreases. \n  * Sorption efficiency is high for a compound with high hydrophobicity. Sorption typically increases when the carbon chain number increases.\n  * Surfactants can form foam when gas is applied to the water. The ability to form foam increases when the carbon chain number increases. Short-chain PFAS are less effective at forming foams. \n  * PFAS also demonstrate volatility and short-chain PFAS can have higher volatility.\n\n## **Impact on Regulation**\nA number of states have established groundwater and drinking water s",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:738:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2772:      "content": "Regulatory classification of PFAS by chain length establishes foundational boundaries that directly govern how environmental fate and transport assessments are conducted in aquifer systems [1]. In addition, the classification hinges on bioaccumulation potential rather than chemical functionality alone [1]. In addition, this bioaccumulation-centered framework creates consequential implications for leaching assessments, as compounds with greater bioaccumulative capacity exhibit fundamentally different subsurface mobility profiles [1].\n\nThe fluorinated carbon count serves as the primary quantitative delimiter between regulatory categories [2]. Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) containing fewer than seven fluorinated carbon atoms and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSAs) with fewer than six fluorinated carbon atoms receive short-chain designation [2]. In addition, these numerical thresholds—six and seven carbons—emerged from empirical observations correlating molecular size with biological uptake efficiency [1]. While boundary selection originated from metabolic and toxicological research, these same structural parameters that govern bioaccumulation—fluorinated chain length and hydrophobic character—inadvertently influence hydrogeological transport behavior, a relationship explored in subsequent treatment and remediation discussions.\n\nThis classification architecture produces asymmetric regulatory coverage that affects aquifer monitoring priorities. Moreover, long-chain compounds, by virtue of their classification, trigger stricter regulatory thresholds and mandatory detection protocols. The bioaccumulation-based framework implicitly assumes that compounds with lower bioaccumulation potential pose reduced human health risks via dietary exposure, an assumption that proves problematic when evaluating groundwater contamination scenarios where drinking water ingestion constitutes the primary exposure pathway. However, short-chain PFAS demonstrate elevated aqueous mobility and diminished sorption affinity, properties that facilitate rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones into saturated aquifer materials, as detailed in Sediment Contamination Hotspots regarding their preferential distribution in distal groundwater positions.\n\nThe structural determinants of bioaccumulation parallel—but do not perfectly align with—the structural controls on subsurface transport. Nevertheless, both phenomena respond to fluorinated chain length, yet the optimal chain length for biological membrane partitioning differs from that governing mineral surface interactions. These divergent structure-activity relationships between biological and geochemical systems create treatment efficiency disparities that complicate remediation design, as elaborated in Treatment Efficiency Disparities.\n\nRegulatory thresholds established under this framework may underestimate the aquifer contamination risk posed by short-chain compounds. In addition, when monitoring programs prioritize long-chain PFAS detection based on bioaccumulation classifications, they potentially overlook the primary mobile contaminants driving plume expansion. The classification system's origin in toxicological rather than hydrogeological science creates methodological tensions that persist throughout environmental assessment protocols.\n\nThe six-carbon and seven-carbon boundaries also influence how precursor compounds are evaluated [2]. Nevertheless, polyfluorinated substances with chain lengths below these thresholds may transform into short-chain perfluorinated products, yet regulatory frameworks often treat precursors separately from their terminal degradation products. This fragmentation complicates source attribution in aquifer systems where biotic or abiotic transformation generates short-chain PFAS from longer-chain parent compounds.\n\nInternational regulatory divergence further complicates classification-based assessment. Moreover, while the six- and seven-carbon thresholds predominate in North American frameworks, European jurisdictions have adopted alternative boundary definitions or compound-specific approaches. Additionally, these disparities generate inconsistent monitoring datasets that hinder comparative leaching potential analysis across hydrogeological settings. Standardized classification would facilitate more robust cross-system transport modeling, though consensus remains elusive given competing scientific and economic interests.\n\nThe relationship between molecular structure and regulatory classification extends beyond simple carbon counting to encompass branching patterns, ether linkages, and terminal functional group variations. Furthermore, these structural modifications alter bioaccumulation potential without necessarily changing the nominal chain length, creating classification ambiguities that propagate into environmental monitoring requirements. Aquifer systems receiving mixed industrial discharges thus may contain PFAS species whose regulatory status remains uncertain, complicating compliance determination and remedial prioritization.\n\nAs discussed in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability, the classification framework's emphasis on bioaccumulation rather than persistence creates additional assessment challenges. In contrast, the \"forever chemical\" paradigm applies with approximately equal force to both short- and long-chain compounds, yet their differential transport properties ensure that long-chain PFAS accumulate in near-source soils while short-chain homologs migrate to distal aquifer positions. This spatial separation means that bioaccumulation-based regulatory thresholds may adequately protect ecological receptors at the surface while failing to safeguard deeper groundwater resources.\n\nThe classification boundaries established by regulatory frameworks require careful contextual interpretation when applied to aquifer vulnerability assessment. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon thresholds represent biologically derived conventions that must be supplemented with hydrogeological transport parameters to accurately predict leaching potential. In addition, subsequent sections examine how these structural classifications translate into measurable differences in environmental partitioning, surface activity, and ultimately treatment efficacy.\n\nThe regulatory fixation on carbon-count thresholds overlooks critical molecular geometry parameters that modulate both bioaccumulation and transport behavior. Linear perfluorinated chains exhibit stronger hydrophobic cohesion and membrane partitioning efficiency compared to branched isomers of identical carbon number, yet regulatory frameworks typically do not discriminate by structural isomerism. Moreover, this omission creates classification paradoxes where branched long-chain PFAS may demonstrate reduced bioaccumulation while retaining substantial sorption affinity, whereas linear short-chain compounds show the inverse pattern. The failure to incorporate three-dimensional molecular configuration into classification schemes introduces systematic uncertainty into aquifer vulnerability assessments.\n\nEther-linked perfluoropolyethers present additional classification challenges that regulatory boundaries struggle to accommodate. Additionally, these compounds incorporate oxygen atoms within the fluorinated backbone, effectively interrupting the continuous carbon chain that carbon-counting methodologies presume. Regulatory frameworks diverge in their treatment of such heteroatomic insertions—some jurisdictions count only contiguous perfluorinated segments, while others tally total fluorinated carbons regardless of intervening heteroatoms. In addition, this inconsistency produces compound-specific regulatory status variations that complicate multinational facility compliance and obscure comparative leaching studies across international aquifer systems. The bioaccumulation potential of ether-linked structures deviates unpredictably from their carbon-count analogs due to altered membrane permeability and metabolic transformation pathways.\n\nTemporal dynamics of precursor transformation introduce classification instability that static regulatory thresholds cannot capture. Moreover, polyfluorinated precursors classified separately from their perfluorinated degradation products persist in aquifer systems for years before abiotic or biotic conversion yields short-chain terminal products [2]. Regulatory frameworks that assess precursors and products independently fail to account for this temporal cascade, resulting in monitoring data that misidentifies the true contaminant mass balance. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon boundaries, derived from steady-state toxicological assessments, prove poorly suited to dynamic systems where precursor loading continuously replenishes short-chain PFAS concentrations.\n\nThe bioaccumulation-centered classification framework neglects groundwater-specific exposure dynamics that diverge markedly from surface water and dietary pathways. In addition, aquifer contamination scenarios emphasize direct ingestion and inhalation of volatilized compounds during showering—routes where short-chain PFAS bioaccumulation irrelevance provides no risk reduction. Regulatory thresholds calibrated to dietary bioaccumulation factors misalign with the actual exposure profiles determining human health outcomes in groundwater-dependent populations. This pathway-specific mismatch represents a fundamental structural limitation of classification systems developed primarily for ecological risk assessment.\n\nEmerging perfluoroalkyl ether carboxylic acids and sulfonic acids—replacement compounds developed under regulatory pressure—exemplify these classification inadequacies. Additionally, these substances often straddle carbon-count boundaries or incorporate novel structural motifs that challenge existing categorization schemes. In contrast, their bioaccumulation profiles remain incompletely characterized, yet their production volumes and environmental releases continue expanding. Regulatory frameworks thus face persistent classification lag relative to industrial chemical innovation, with aquifer systems serving as unintended receptors for substances whose transport behavior and health significance remain scientifically unresolved.",

[Events](https://www.sanbornhead.com/category/events/)\n\n  * [Contact Us](https://www.sanbornhead.com/contact-us/)\n  * [Client Login](https://www.sanbornhead.com/client-login/)\n\n# Short or Long? PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate\nBy: [Lingke Zeng, LEED AP](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=2543) and [Steve Zemba, PhD, PE](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=1007) on August 24, 2022 \n![](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/AdobeStock_429831073-675x490.jpeg)\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, known as PFAS, are the most well-known emerging contaminants and have become an urgent environmental issue in recent years. PFAS have been detected in [landfill leachate](https://www.sanbornhead.com/keep-those-landfill-liquids-flowing/) at levels above drinking water standards. Although PFAS treatment is not currently required for landfill leachate, the progress in PFAS regulation at the national level suggests that PFAS standards in surface water will be established and landfill leachate treatment will be required.\nAs landfill operators contemplate changes in environmental regulations, it should be noted that not all PFAS contaminants behave in the same way. In fact, examining the compound chain length of PFAS will help us to identify which compounds require additional treatment to eliminate their impacts on the environment.\n## PFAS **Chain Length Impact on Contamination**\nBased on the carbon chain number, perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) can be divided into short-chain and long-chain compounds, as shown in Table 1. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS is based on the bioaccumulation potential of the individual compound. The long-chain PFAS typically show high bioaccumulation potential. For perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), with a carboxylic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than seven. For perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), with a sulfonic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than five. The chain definition of polyfluoroalkyl substances is not well defined but generally follows a similar trend as PFAAs. \n![Table showing short- and long-chain PFAA classifications from ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Table-1-recreated-1024x160.png)Table 1: PFAA short- and long-chain classification (Source from [ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS released in February 2021](https://pfas-1.itrcweb.org/))\n## **Landfill Leachate Composition**\nHalf of the total PFAS in landfill leachate can be contributed by polyfluoroalkyl substances, especially 5:3 Fluorotelomer carboxylic acid (5:3 FTCA) – a compound not on most analytical lists. However, typically only PFAAs are regulated and most landfills have been collecting PFAAs and a limited number of polyfluoroalkyl substances. Figure 1 demonstrates the PFAAs composition in an example landfill. At either the upper or lower limits of the total concentration range, the short-chain PFAS are the dominant group. Although perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) is listed as short-chain in Table 1, its characteristic is in-line with long-chain and grouped with long-chain PFAS.\n![Charts comparing high and low concentrations of PFAS composition in leachate at an example landfill](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Figure1-1024x403.png)Figure 1: PFAS Composition at an example landfill.  \nLeft – Composition at lowest combined concentration; Right – Composition at highest combined concentration\n## PFAS **Chain Length on Chemical and Physical Characteristics**\nAll PFAS, being surfactants, contain a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic function head. Long-chain PFAS show more hydrophobicity (dislike water) and short-chain PFAS show more hydrophilicity (like water). Therefore,\n  * Higher solubility is often observed for a compound with high hydrophilicity. Solubility typically increases when the carbon chain number decreases. \n  * Sorption efficiency is high for a compound with high hydrophobicity. Sorption typically increases when the carbon chain number increases.\n  * Surfactants can form foam when gas is applied to the water. The ability to form foam increases when the carbon chain number increases. Short-chain PFAS are less effective at forming foams. \n  * PFAS also demonstrate volatility and short-chain PFAS can have higher volatility.\n\n## **Impact on Regulation**\nA number of states have established groundwater and drinking water s",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:744:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3912:  "draft_report": "# Differential Leaching Potential of Long-Chain versus Short-Chain PFAS in Aquifer Systems: Transport Mechanisms, Environmental Partitioning, and Treatment Implications\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis report examines the differential leaching potential of long-chain versus short-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) within aquifer systems, synthesizing regulatory frameworks and scientific evidence regarding transport mechanisms, environmental partitioning, and treatment efficacy. The analysis encompasses the physicochemical determinants of chain-length specific mobility, including air-water interfacial dynamics, soil-porewater partitioning, and groundwater-surface water exchange processes, alongside comparative assessment of remediation technology performance across chain lengths. The scope is limited to environmental fate and transport within subsurface hydrological systems, explicitly excluding manufacturing source characterization, human toxicological dose-response relationships, ecological risk assessments beyond bioaccumulation potential, and atmospheric long-range transport modeling beyond immediate air-water exchange mechanisms. Key assumptions include the treatment of PFAS as environmentally persistent contaminants with degradation-resistant properties, and the reliance on existing regulatory classifications based on fluorinated carbon chain length as proxies for bioaccumulation potential and subsurface mobility. Limitations include the moderate strength of evidence regarding sediment hotspot dynamics and secondary source contributions, the predominance of ex-situ treatment efficiency data over in-situ aquifer transport studies, and potential geographic biases in groundwater monitoring datasets, which may constrain predictive certainty regarding long-term leaching scenarios under varying hydrogeological conditions.\n\n## Regulatory Classification Frameworks: Defining Chain-Length Boundaries and Bioaccumulation Criteria\n\n Regulatory classification of PFAS by chain length establishes foundational boundaries that directly govern how environmental fate and transport assessments are conducted in aquifer systems [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The classification hinges on bioaccumulation potential rather than chemical functionality alone [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This bioaccumulation-centered framework creates consequential implications for leaching assessments, as compounds with greater bioaccumulative capacity exhibit fundamentally different subsurface mobility profiles [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe fluorinated carbon count serves as the primary quantitative delimiter between regulatory categories [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) containing fewer than seven fluorinated carbon atoms and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSAs) with fewer than six fluorinated carbon atoms receive short-chain designation [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. These numerical thresholds—six and seven carbons—emerged from empirical observations correlating molecular size with biological uptake efficiency [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. While boundary selection originated from metabolic and toxicological research, these same structural parameters that govern bioaccumulation—fluorinated chain length and hydrophobic character—inadvertently influence hydrogeological transport behavior, a relationship explored in subsequent treatment and remediation discussions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThis classification architecture produces asymmetric regulatory coverage that affects aquifer monitoring priorities [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Long-chain compounds, by virtue of their classification, trigger stricter regulatory thresholds and mandatory detection protocols [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The bioaccumulation-based framework implicitly assumes that compounds with lower bioaccumulation potential pose reduced human health risks via dietary exposure, an assumption that proves problematic when evaluating groundwater contamination scenarios where drinking water ingestion constitutes the primary exposure pathway [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate elevated aqueous mobility and diminished sorption affinity, properties that facilitate rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones into saturated aquifer materials [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea], as detailed in Sediment Contamination Hotspots regarding their preferential distribution in distal groundwater positions.\n\nThe structural determinants of bioaccumulation parallel—but do not perfectly align with—the structural controls on subsurface transport [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Both phenomena respond to fluorinated chain length, yet the optimal chain length for biological membrane partitioning differs from that governing mineral surface interactions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These divergent structure-activity relationships between biological and geochemical systems create treatment efficiency disparities that complicate remediation design, as elaborated in Treatment Efficiency Disparities [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nRegulatory thresholds established under this framework consequently may underestimate the aquifer contamination risk posed by short-chain compounds [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. When monitoring programs prioritize long-chain PFAS detection based on bioaccumulation classifications, they potentially overlook the primary mobile contaminants driving plume expansion [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The classification system's origin in toxicological rather than hydrogeological science creates methodological tensions that persist throughout environmental assessment protocols [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe six-carbon and seven-carbon boundaries also influence how precursor compounds are evaluated [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. Polyfluorinated substances with chain lengths below these thresholds may transform into short-chain perfluorinated products, yet regulatory frameworks often treat precursors separately from their terminal degradation products [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This fragmentation complicates source attribution in aquifer systems where biotic or abiotic transformation generates short-chain PFAS from longer-chain parent compounds [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nInternational regulatory divergence further complicates classification-based assessment [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. While the six- and seven-carbon thresholds predominate in North American frameworks, European jurisdictions have adopted alternative boundary definitions or compound-specific approaches [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These disparities generate inconsistent monitoring datasets that hinder comparative leaching potential analysis across hydrogeological settings [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Standardized classification would facilitate more robust cross-system transport modeling, though consensus remains elusive given competing scientific and economic interests [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe relationship between molecular structure and regulatory classification extends beyond simple carbon counting to encompass branching patterns, ether linkages, and terminal functional group variations [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These structural modifications alter bioaccumulation potential without necessarily changing the nominal chain length, creating classification ambiguities that propagate into environmental monitoring requirements [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Aquifer systems receiving mixed industrial discharges thus may contain PFAS species whose regulatory status remains uncertain, complicating compliance determination and remedial prioritization [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nAs discussed in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability, the classification framework's emphasis on bioaccumulation rather than persistence creates additional assessment challenges [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The \"forever chemical\" paradigm applies with approximately equal force to both short- and long-chain compounds, yet their differential transport properties ensure that long-chain PFAS accumulate in near-source soils while short-chain homologs migrate to distal aquifer positions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This spatial separation means that bioaccumulation-based regulatory thresholds may adequately protect ecological receptors at the surface while failing to safeguard deeper groundwater resources [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe classification boundaries established by regulatory frameworks consequently require careful contextual interpretation when applied to aquifer vulnerability assessment [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The six- and seven-carbon thresholds represent biologically derived conventions that must be supplemented with hydrogeological transport parameters to accurately predict leaching potential [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Subsequent sections examine how these structural classifications translate into measurable differences in environmental partitioning, surface activity, and ultimately treatment efficacy [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\n## Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability: The Forever Chemical Paradigm\n\n The carbon-fluorine bond imparts thermodynamic and kinetic stability that prevents hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial transformation pathways typical of conventional organic contaminants [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. This structural integrity ensures that PFAS remain chemically intact in subsurface environments rather than degrading into benign products [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. The classification of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" reflects this exceptional resistance to degradation across environmental matrices [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nChain-length differentiation governs how persistence manifests in aquifer systems, as detailed in the examination of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially reduced interfacial accumulation [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396], remaining predominantly aqueous-phase and facilitating more rapid transport to saturated zones—a mobility pattern addressed in the analysis of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. The persistence characteristic thus translates into divergent contamination architectures: long-chain compounds establish retarded but enduring source zones in vadose soils, while short-chain analogs generate rapidly disseminating plumes with limited formation of retrievable contaminant masses [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nThe relationship between soil-phase and aqueous-phase concentrations directly governs leaching potential to underlying groundwater [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain compounds, with their stronger sorptive affinity and interfacial accumulation, exhibit extended residence times in unsaturated soils before groundwater impact—a temporal delay explored in Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones, yet their equivalent chemical stability ensures decades to centuries of aquifer contamination once mobilized [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. Short-chain persistence combined with higher mobility produces faster aquifer penetration with equally protracted contamination duration [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\nLong-chain PFAS demonstrate pronounced bioaccumulation in aquatic organisms, effectively transferring from water column to biological tissues and potentially delaying groundwater recovery even after source removal [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. This biological persistence creates an additional compartment for contaminant storage and subsequent release. Short-chain PFAS, while exhibiting reduced bioconcentration factors, maintain sufficient environmental persistence to ensure continuous water column availability [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nNatural attenuation proves nonviable as a management strategy for either chain-length category. Unlike petroleum hydrocarbons or chlorinated solvents where transformation products may exhibit reduced toxicity or mobility, PFAS degradation—when it occurs—produces terminal perfluorinated acids that retain environmental persistence and often equivalent or greater mobility than parent compounds [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. This absence of attenuation products suitable for natural recovery forces reliance on engineered removal systems [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. Conventional pump-and-treat remediation assumes contaminant mass depletion over operational timeframes, yet PFAS persistence ensures that extracted water concentrations remain stable absent physical removal mechanisms [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. Treatment system operation must extend to decadal scales rather than the years typical for biodegradable contaminants [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\nThe stability characteristics redefine aquifer vulnerability assessment by eliminating recovery timelines and natural attenuation capacity from decision frameworks [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. The differential expression of persistence between chain lengths—mobility-versus-retention trade-offs—determines specific aquifer risk profiles but not the fundamental requirement for permanent institutional controls [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. These persistence properties interact with transport mechanisms to produce contamination patterns that distinguish PFAS from all other groundwater pollutant classes [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\n## Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Compartments\n\nPFAS contamination spans water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations across national boundaries, establishing the environmental ubiquity that enables differential leaching behaviors between chain-length categories [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. This pervasive distribution creates interconnected pathways through which surface-released compounds migrate toward subsurface aquifer systems.\n\nThe relationship between soil-bound PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly governs groundwater contamination potential [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Research examining unsaturated zone mass transfer reveals that soil-phase measurements alone inadequately predict leaching risk; rather, the concentration gradient between retained and mobile fractions determines vertical transport kinetics [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This partitioning behavior varies systematically with perfluorinated chain length.\n\nField quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water addresses a critical literature gap in understanding compartmental exchange dynamics [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da]. Bidirectional flux occurs near manufacturing facilities, with contaminated groundwater discharging to surface water while surface infiltration simultaneously recharges aquifers [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da]. This hydrological connectivity ensures that surface water contamination propagates through river networks and reservoir infrastructure into underlying groundwater reserves.\n\nLong-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS accumulate substantially at air-water interfaces, exceeding the interfacial affinity of short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential interfacial partitioning has direct implications for subsurface transport: stronger air-water interfacial adsorption retards long-chain compound mobility through unsaturated soils, while short-chain compounds with diminished interfacial affinity migrate more rapidly through vadose zones [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. The measured and simulated data confirm that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil matrices [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06].\n\nGroundwater concentration ranges demonstrate the practical outcome of these transport dynamics. Total PFAS concentrations in natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film-forming foam reached approximately 15,000 ng L−1, indicating substantial subsurface migration capacity despite chain-length-dependent retention mechanisms [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. In urbanized and agricultural settings, groundwater samples exhibited total PFAS concentrations varying between 22 and 718 ng L−1, confirming widespread aquifer contamination across diverse land-use contexts [CITE:ev_896aaf3a3fe097c9].\n\nThe classification framework distinguishing short-chain from long-chain PFAS derives from bioaccumulation potential rather than transport behavior alone [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This regulatory distinction carries environmental partitioning implications: compounds with elevated bioaccumulation factors typically exhibit stronger hydrophobic and lipophilic characteristics that enhance soil retention while potentially delaying groundwater arrival. Conversely, short-chain replacements with reduced bioaccumulation potential demonstrate correspondingly diminished subsurface attenuation.\n\nAtmospheric transport extends contamination beyond point-source footprints, enabling precipitation-recharge area contamination distant from industrial or firefighting application sites [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. This aerial dispersion mechanism creates geographically distributed source areas that complicate aquifer protection strategies, particularly for short-chain compounds with elevated vapor pressures [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. The atmospheric pathway's contribution to background contamination levels challenges conventional source-control approaches.\n\nSoil profiles function as critical transition zones where differential retention between chain-length categories manifests. Long-chain compounds exhibit stronger sorption in surface soils and sediments, creating delayed but persistent groundwater contamination potential [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Short-chain compounds demonstrate reduced sorption affinity, facilitating more rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. These contrasting temporal pathways yield distinct concentration profiles in receiving aquifers.\n\nThe nationwide distribution patterns establish that aquifer vulnerability assessment must transcend source identification to emphasize transport pathway analysis and compound-specific mobility prediction [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. Contamination ubiquity means both long-chain and short-chain PFAS possess demonstrated capacity to reach groundwater systems, but through mechanistically distinct routes that govern exposure timing and magnitude.\n\n## Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Fractionation and Solution Retention\n\n Long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain congeners, establishing fundamental boundary conditions for foam-based separation efficacy in aquifer remediation [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential interfacial partitioning directly determines susceptibility to foam fractionation, a physical separation technology increasingly deployed for PFAS-impacted groundwater [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nFoam fractionation exploits the tendency of surfactant-like molecules to concentrate at air-water interfaces and subsequently become entrained within rising foam bubbles [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The molecular architecture underlying this disparity involves increased fluorinated carbon chain length amplifying hydrophobic character while maintaining hydrophilic functional groups, creating the amphiphilic structure essential for interfacial partitioning [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Short-chain PFAS, with truncated fluorinated tails, exhibit diminished hydrophobicity and consequently reduced propensity for air-water interface accumulation [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThis differential surface activity generates pronounced treatment efficacy disparities. CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation reduced total PFAS concentrations in natural AFFF-impacted groundwaters from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes, demonstrating substantial removal capacity for surface-active long-chain compounds [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate incomplete removal despite extended treatment durations, their limited surface activity preventing effective enrichment within foam phases [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Consequently, groundwater systems contaminated with short-chain PFAS or mixed chain-length assemblages typical of industrial precursor degradation experience incomplete treatment through foam fractionation alone [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThe physicochemical drivers underlying this differential behavior extend beyond simple hydrophobicity metrics. Solution retention characteristics—the propensity for PFAS to remain dissolved rather than partition into separable phases—depend upon complex interactions between molecular structure, water chemistry, and interfacial dynamics [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Long-chain PFAS readily depart bulk solution through foam entrainment, whereas short-chain PFAS resist such partitioning, maintaining higher aqueous concentrations and exhibiting enhanced mobility within subsurface systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones). Research on unsaturated soils demonstrates the importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil matrices, with implications for porewater mobility and potential groundwater migration [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06] (see also: Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Differential Accumulation by Chain Length).\n\nThese observations illuminate fundamental constraints on foam-based remediation strategies. Technologies optimized for long-chain PFAS removal underperform when applied to short-chain-dominated contamination scenarios [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The solution retention exhibited by short-chain compounds ensures their continued presence in groundwater, facilitating transport through porous media and expanding contaminant plumes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Treatment system design must account for this chain-length-dependent fractionation susceptibility, potentially necessitating sequential or hybrid approaches addressing both compound classes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThe connection between surface activity and leaching potential operates through multiple pathways. Reduced foam fractionation efficiency for short-chain PFAS implies these compounds persist longer in aqueous phases, sustaining driving gradients for vertical migration through unsaturated zones [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Chain Lengths). Meanwhile, long-chain PFAS removed through interfacial processes undergo reconcentration in foamate, requiring subsequent treatment or disposal that introduces secondary management challenges [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The differential solution retention characteristics thus shape not only immediate treatment outcomes but also long-term contaminant fate and transport trajectories within aquifer systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\n## Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones\n\n The vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements.\n\nWhile interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]—a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. Short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area.\n\nSorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]—consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nThe relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nVadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nSites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology.\n\nTreatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Remediation strategies targeting the vadose zone must incorporate chain-length-specific extraction protocols rather than assuming uniform contaminant behavior across PFAS classes [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\n## Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Source Potential\n\n Sediment contamination hotspots function as persistent secondary sources capable of sustaining aquifer recontamination over extended temporal scales. These hotspots form preferentially near the sediment surface in zones immediately downstream from point source discharges, creating localized zones of elevated PFAA concentration that persist beyond primary source remediation [CITE:ev_9b490b7f35013689]. This surface-proximal localization carries particular significance for aquifer vulnerability, as these shallow reservoirs maintain direct hydraulic connectivity with underlying groundwater systems through hyporheic exchange and vertical infiltration pathways—a dynamic explored in detail in the discussion of soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones.\n\nThe secondary source potential of sediment hotspots derives from their capacity to release accumulated PFAS mass following perturbations to prevailing geochemical conditions. Unlike primary sources where contamination inputs occur through direct discharge, secondary release from sediments operates through desorption-driven mechanisms triggered by changes in porewater chemistry, flow regime alterations, or competitive displacement by co-contaminants—processes that parallel the partitioning dynamics governing PFAS mobility in unsaturated soil systems. This distinction fundamentally shapes remediation prioritization, as hotspot sediments require active intervention rather than passive source control measures.\n\nLong-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially stronger sediment retention and more efficient hotspot formation near discharge points, where these compounds accumulate to higher concentrations within smaller spatial footprints. Conversely, short-chain PFAS demonstrate greater downstream transport capacity, distributing contamination across broader sediment reaches rather than concentrating in discrete hotspots. The bioaccumulation-based classification scheme previously established for PFAS categorization reinforces this functional divergence in environmental partitioning behavior.\n\nThe implications for aquifer recontamination risk are substantial. Long-chain PFAS hotspots represent high-concentration, spatially constrained sources; however, the magnitude of flux to underlying aquifers depends on site-specific hydraulic gradients and mass transfer kinetics rather than concentration alone, reflecting the complex relationship between soil-phase concentrations and groundwater loading documented in soil-porewater partitioning research. Short-chain PFAS, despite forming less pronounced hotspots, create diffuse contamination zones that sustain chronic low-level loading across extended groundwater capture areas. Neither scenario permits simple risk assessment based solely on total mass calculations; rather, the spatial configuration of contamination relative to groundwater flow paths determines secondary source significance. Research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data addresses critical gaps in understanding these transport dynamics [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da].\n\nTreatment implications diverge markedly between hotspot types. Long-chain enriched sediments may respond to targeted extraction technologies leveraging their strong surface affinity. Foam fractionation techniques have demonstrated capacity to reduce total PFAS concentrations in natural groundwaters from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below detection limits of 8 ng L−1 within 15 minutes, suggesting applicability for concentrated hotspot remediation [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. Short-chain contaminated sediments present substantially greater technical challenges given their weak retention across environmental matrices, necessitating more aggressive and costly remediation approaches. The differential treatability of hotspot sediments reinforces the economic and technical advantages of preventing long-chain PFAS discharge relative to managing distributed short-chain contamination.\n\nTemporal dynamics of hotspot reactivation warrant particular attention in aquifer protection planning. Sediment PFAS reservoirs can maintain release potential over decadal timescales, substantially exceeding the operational lifetimes of typical source control measures. This persistence creates legacy contamination scenarios where aquifer systems continue receiving PFAS loading decades after primary source cessation, complicating attainment of groundwater quality objectives. The chain-length composition of hotspot sediments directly influences this temporal dimension, with long-chain compounds exhibiting slower release kinetics that prolong secondary source activity.\n\nUnderstanding hotspot formation and secondary source potential provides essential context for interpreting groundwater concentration baselines, particularly in distinguishing true background conditions from legacy contamination signals. Elevated aquifer PFAS concentrations in areas lacking identifiable current sources frequently reflect sediment hotspot reactivation rather than undetected ongoing discharge—a consideration central to establishing defensible groundwater concentration baselines. This recognition shifts monitoring priorities toward sediment characterization and hyporheic zone assessment in groundwater protection strategies.\n\n## Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Differential Accumulation by Chain Length\n\n Air-water interfacial partitioning establishes a primary mechanism by which chain length governs PFAS transport through unsaturated zones. Long-chain compounds including PFOA and PFOS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than their short-chain counterparts PFBS and PFBA [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential accumulation arises from amplified amphiphilic character with chain extension—longer fluorocarbon tails increasingly orient toward non-aqueous phases while charged head groups remain water-anchored, creating thermodynamically favorable conditions for interface residence [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nThe environmental significance of this interfacial accumulation extends to soil distribution patterns. Research examining PFAS partitioning between bulk soil matrices and porewater demonstrates that air-water interfacial adsorption governs long-chain PFAS localization within soil samples [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Soil pores contain extensive interfacial area, particularly under intermediate moisture conditions, providing substantial retention capacity that operates independently of mineral surface sorption or organic matter partitioning [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. This mechanism represents an additional partitioning pathway that short-chain compounds largely cannot exploit [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nLong-chain PFAS do not merely experience transport retardation; they partition into distinct physical domains that fundamentally alter migration pathways. The air-water interface functions as a temporary reservoir that buffers long-chain compounds against rapid leaching while simultaneously generating concentration gradients that drive lateral diffusion and non-equilibrium transport behaviors [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06].
