.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5242:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_015917.json:1924:      "content": "Title: Pull-Off Adhesion Test for Coatings on Large Pipes: possible variations in failure location and mode URL Source: https://www.nwpipe.com/app/uploads/2020/08/Adhesion-Testing-Croll.pdf Published Time: Wed, 26 Feb 2025 15:14:35 GMT Number of Pages: 15 Markdown Content: Pull-Off Adhesion Test for Coatings on Large Pipes: possible variations in failure location and mode. S. G. Croll 1 , C. Siripirom 1 , B. D. Keil 21 North Dakota State University, Dept. of Coatings and Polymeric Materials, P.O. Box 6050, Dept 2760, Fargo, ND 58108-6050. PH (701) 231-9415. E-mail: Stuart.Croll@ndsu.edu 2 Northwest Pipe Company, 5721 SE Columbia Way, Suite 200, Vancouver, WA 98661. PH (360) 397-6343. E-mail: bkeil@nwpipe.com Abstract Pull-off adhesion testing per ASTM D-4541 is a commonly used quality control check for coatings on large diameter steel water pipe. During the testing a metal dolly is glued to a pipeline coating then pulled off, to assess adhesion of the coating. In practice, results are very sensitive to circumstances and have large standard deviations compared to their mean value. It is clear from a simple inspection of Griffith\u2019s equation for the strength of materials that the pull-off stress at failure increases with the stiffness of the materials, the interfacial failure energy and is diminished by presence of existing cracks or flaws. Finite element stress analysis explored the effect of the coating and glue stiffness as well as some of the other possible reasons for variation in the results, i.e. pipe curvature, misalignment of the dolly, as well as the effect of scoring through the coating and glue around the dolly. The tensile modulus of three polyurethane coatings, an epoxy adhesive and a cyanoacrylate adhesive were measured and showed that it was possible for a polyurethane coating to be stiffer than an adhesive used to fix the dolly in place. The location of the maximum strain, in the glue or the coating, was used as an indicator of where the adhesive failure was likely to occur within the overall joint. Results indicate that the influence of dolly misalignment on the coated pipe is greater than the influence of the pipe curvature itself. If the dolly was aligned perfectly, the greatest strain, and thus most likely location of failure was on the crest of the pipe at the pipe-coating interface. The value of the strain was not a strong function of the curvature of the underlying pipe surface. If the dolly was misaligned, then the locus of failure shifted to where the glue was thinnest, but remained at the coating-pipe interface, if the glue is stiffer than the coating. However, if the coating was stiffer than the glue, then the location of the > 1319 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 greatest strain indicated that failure was more likely to be at the glue-dolly junction in all cases, rather than at the coating-pipe interface. Users should be aware of how these, and other possible, variations affect the pull-off results if they rely upon single dolly pull values to assess the overall adhesion or use the test method to assess the likely reliability of a coated steel pipe in service. Introduction Steel pipelines used to transport water often have large fractions of their length buried underground, or otherwise inaccessible. One of the principal threats to the long term life of the pipeline is corrosion of the steel, so external polyurethane coatings are applied to prevent water and dissolved salts from attacking the pipes. Pull-off adhesion testing of the coating to the pipe, according to ASTM D-4541, is a universally used procedure to check the quality of the coating and its application to a properly prepared steel pipe. If the values obtained meet a standard, for example AWWA C222, then depending on the results of other tests, the coated pipe is deemed suitable for long term service. The pull-off adhesion test is notorious for the variation in the results [Devries 2002, Ramos 2012, Croll 2012] and this paper explores some of the reasons for the variation. In practice, the test is performed just after the pipe sections are coated, in the factory, or in storage, or at the job site. Thus the test is performed under environmental conditions that vary from cold to hot, humid or dry, and so on. Commonly, the test is performed where the engineer can reach, which might be on the side of a large diameter pipe. One can already appreciate the difficulty in obtaining reproducible results. Causes of variability include problems in surface preparation, adhesive application, curing, thickness and evenness of adhesive, alignment of the pull-off dolly and variation introduced by the operator of the test equipment. The investigation here focuses on two forms of misalignment; when the substrate is not flat, as is specified in ASTM D-4541, and when the dolly is not set parallel to the substrate. The need to maintain perfect alignment in the pull-off test has been realized for many years [Anderson 1 1988] where it is suggested that good techniques can reduce the standard deviation to 10% of the mean, or less. This has also been suggested in other publications dealing with detailed stress analysis of adhesive joints [Anderson 2 1988]. But, clearly, normal practice makes this difficult to achieve. Large differences in values are seen, even using the same materials, but at different institutions [Ikegami, 1996][ASTM 2009]. There are two complementary approaches to understanding the strength of a material or an adhesive joint. The energy approach, like other thermodynamic approaches, indicates whether a change, here the adhesive failure, may occur. This is often the > 1320 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 simplest approach and may be all that is necessary. Stress analysis is much more complicated, but may be necessary because it provides the mechanistic information, indicating where the failure is most likely to start and progress. Analyses of material systems, including adhesive joints, assume that the adhesive materials are homogenous and continuous. Typical coatings contain pigment particles, extender particles, may be phase separated and exhibit other inhomogeneities. In most discussions of adhesion, a coating or adhesive is assumed to be a single, deformable homogenous layer between two rigid adherends, and that the average stress across the area of the joint is sufficient to characterize the strength of the joint. In testing adhesion of coatings, the glue used to adhere the dolly to the coating is usually assumed to be part of the dolly and not to require consideration in the analysis. This implies that it must be very rigid compared to the coating, as well as adhere very well. Except in the most detailed analyses, the materials that form the joint are assumed to obey Hooke\u2019s law, i.e. be linear and elastic in their mechanical properties. In contrast, polymeric coatings and adhesives are intrinsically non-linear, viscoelastic materials. Thermodynamic Approach to Adhesion Failure Griffith\u2019s equation [Griffith 1921] for the strength of materials was a very important advance since it recognized that most material\u2019s strength was limited by cracks and flaws that were already present. The equation describes how brittle failure depends on the properties of materials and that failure is due to enlargement of a crack or flaw. A crack in this context may be actual damage or an interface where the adhesion between polymer and a pigment particle, for example, is very weak. 2 External Built in E Stress Stress a ## \u03b3\u03c3 \u03c0 \u2212= = + Where: E = Young\u2019s modulus of the material \u03b3 = Interfacial energy of adhesion/area = energy required to create new crack surface within a material or interface (strain energy release rate) a = radius of the existing crack (assuming a circular shape) \u03c3 = Griffith fracture stress, the critical stress above which brittle failure occurs as a crack propagates, this is usually applied externally, but if the materials already have stress built in, from their curing chemistry, for example, the amount of external stress required is less. The tensile modulus is appropriate in this equation because failure usually opens the crack wider, and stretches the material at the crack tip. This equation is often applied to polymer failure because failure usually occurs, suddenly, at very high strain-rates across the crack tip, and polymers are approximately brittle under these circumstances. If the polymer does not behave like a brittle, linear elastic material, then \u03b3 must include the energy dissipated in processes leading up to the crack propagating, e.g. > 1321 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 yielding, fibril formation etc. which can give energies orders of magnitude higher than that for crack propagation only [Shull 2000, Creton 2000, Crosby 2000]. The Griffith equation has been used widely in engineering and physical science, to describe the failure of many materials. When one measures the stress necessary to pull a dolly and the adhering coating away from the substrate, it gives a value for the term on the right hand side of the equation. It does not give a value for \u03b3 which is the parameter relevant for the adhesion and there is no way to separate this parameter without other information. Any internal stress, caused by curing shrinkage or accidents of coating or glue application will diminish the external stress necessary for fracture. Pull-off values can be increased by coatings that have higher values of modulus, have smaller pre-existing cracks and have other mechanisms for dissipating energy within the materials. Adding a filler to a polymer increases the mechanical modulus and may increase the measured pull-off, as pointed out above. However, fillers may agglomerate and diminish the strength considerably because the rupture will be initiated at the agglomerate, i.e. they produced a large Griffith flaw. Large (micro) particles of filler have stress concentrations around them that form at the poles (even if the particle was perfectly spherical without sharp edges) with respect to the stress direction [Fond 2001] so the interface around a filler is a likely starting point for rupture. There is considerable interest in the use of nano-fillers where the particle size is less than the yield zone of the polymer and so one should get the advantage of the increase in modulus without the disadvantage of causing a large flaw that initiates failure. Unfortunately, nano-particles are very difficult to disperse so they can form agglomerates that are much larger and so form larger flaws and so lose the advantage. The pull-off test for adhesion often produces a failure within the coating or within the glue, or at an interface or failure that changes its location across the overall area of the fracture. In a typical test, we have the coating and glue that may fail in a cohesive sense and we have the coating-substrate, coating-glue, glue-dolly interfaces that may fail in an adhesive sense. The failure will occur at those locations, adhesive or cohesive, that due to their properties offer the least resistance to the external stress. There is a variant by Kendall [Kendall 1971] of the Griffith approach specifically for an adhesive between two perfectly rigid adherends, that ignores the possibility of pre-existing cracks and cohesive failure. It gives a result in a similar form: Kt ## \u03b3 ## \u03c3 = Where: \u03c3 = pull-off adhesion stress > 1322 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 K = bulk modulus of the adhesive layer since it is constrained between rigid bodies and no crack exists \u03b3 = interfacial energy of adhesion/area t = thickness of the adhesive/coating layer Again, the measured pull-off stress is a value determined by a combination of parameters, not solely the interfacial adhesion. The interfacial energy of adhesion must depend on the nature and number of the interactions between the coating material and the material of the substrate. Coatings studied by this test are rarely chemically linked to the substrate, nor interpenetrate the substrate. The interactions between a polymer and a substrate will depend on the polymer chain conformation at the substrate and the polymer moieties that are interacting with the material of the substrate. Polymer modulus will depend on the bulk properties of the composition, including crosslink density, chain stiffness etc. Interfacial interactions are not necessarily dictated by the bulk properties of the coating. Even if the alignment in the pull-off test is perfect and all the other conditions have been met rigorously, the stress distribution along the bond line is not even. It is well known that stress concentrations occur at the edges of joints, at crack tips or other heterogeneities. The path of the failure crack can be understood from the stress intensities and the properties of the materials at the microscopic level. The expenditure of mechanical energy in all the processes leading up to failure will depend, of course, on these stress concentrations and their locations. Average stress across the whole joint area is significantly smaller than that at the stress concentration and since the failure will be determined by the maximum stress, average stress is not useful for correlation with other properties. The energy approach is simple, but it has already shown that in the dolly-glue-coating-substrate system, the pull-off test might produce a variety of results depending on the interfacial or cohesive fracture energies, material stiffnesses, and which material or interface has a crucial flaw. In order to gain more insight about how material properties and test geometry affect the how the adhesive or cohesive failure occurs, it is necessary to examine the stress, or strain, distribution within the system. Stress Distribution Films between a rigid dolly and a rigid substrate, are constrained by adhesion on both sides. Thus there can be a large hydrostatic stress due to this confinement which is largest in the center. This effect will be greater in less compressible materials, with a Poisson\u2019s ratio closer to 0.5, see Figure 1. In practice, the coating thickness is much less than in the cartoon here, so the effect is greater. In reality the coating (and the glue) is not cut perfectly even with the dolly circumference, but extends, somewhat inconsistently, little way beyond. > 1323 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 Coating/adhesive Substrate > Pulling off force Lateral strain > Stresses anywhere in coating are 3-D > Dolly Figure 1. Schematic of the pull-off adhesion test illustrating how constraints produced by adhering to a rigid substrate and a rigid dolly cause multi-directional strains in the deformable coatin"

failure](https://www.biolinscientific.com/hs-fs/hubfs/adhesive%20vs.%20cohesive%20failure.jpg?height=480&name=adhesive%20vs.%20cohesive%20failure.jpg)\n[Susanna Laur\u00e9n](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/author/susanna-laur%C3%A9n) Sep 14, \u201921 > 3 min \n# Cohesive vs. adhesive failure in adhesive bonding\n##  [ ](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog)\nAdhesive bonding is a process where two similar or dissimilar materials are fastened together. As the name implies, in adhesive bonding, an adhesive, typically some kind of glue is used to bond the two surfaces. In the adhesive bonding process, both adhesive and cohesive forces are involved. Depending on the strength of these forces, the adhesion failure can be either adhesive, cohesive, or substrate failure. \n## Adhesive and cohesive forces are involved in the bonding process\nWhen the glue is applied on the surface, its ability to [wet the surface](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/wettability-determines-the-adhesion-of-coatings) is of utmost importance for good adhesion. Good wettability ensures that the glue can penetrate surface structures which leads to better [mechanical interlocking](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/what-is-adhesion) at the interface. The adhesive forces between the glue and the substrate as well as the cohesive forces in the glue determine how well the glue can wet the surface. If the cohesive forces inside the glue (or any other coating formulation for that matter) are high, the glue prefers to stay together rather than interact with the surface. However, if the adhesive interaction between the substrate and the glue is high enough, the glue might spread after all. Once the glue has hardened and the interfaces are formed, these same forces determine whether the bonding is permanent or adhesion failure is likely to happen.\n## Adhesion failure mechanisms\nTo understand why bonding fails, we need to first define the ways the failure can occur. ![Bonding failure_light](https://www.biolinscientific.com/hs-fs/hubfs/images/Biolin%20Scientific/Blog/Bonding%20failure_light.jpg?width=1508&name=Bonding%20failure_light.jpg)These can be divided into three categories:\n  * Adhesive failure \n  * Cohesive failure\n  * Substrate failure \n\nAdhesive failure, or delamination, is one of the most common types of failure mechanisms. There the two dissimilar materials detach from each other. The failure can happen between the adhesive and either of the two substrates, it is bonding together. Cohesive failure happens in the adhesive itself or inside the layer of a coating. Substrate failure is not related to the bonding process itself as it is a problem in a substrate.\nAs you might guess already, the cohesive failure will happen if the cohesive forces inside the adhesive are not strong enough. Then, on the other hand, the adhesive failure happens if the glue has not spread properly on the substrate. To make sure that the spreading of the glue (or other coating formulation) is sufficient, contact angle measurements can be used.\nTo read more about adhesion and how contact angle measurements can help to predict its success, please download the white paper through the link below.\n## [White paper Adhesion - How to Use Contact Angle Measurements to Predict Adhesion and Bonding? Download](https://www.biolinscientific.com/cs/c/?cta_guid=9e9bf7f1-5bc8-4f1c-af24-566e15e1cf68&signature=AAH58kFLTiz9RY7fwCL0SnjhkNOn8idLSw&portal_id=516902&pageId=55198155677&placement_guid=a852e4a4-d28d-4f47-bf75-b5340e0be0e7&click=e4bd2eca-f7b6-4427-b7ee-cc622b5fd633&redirect_url=APefjpHlxVmk_vF_eDTG_LgWBDaQ6vroCNVT75qMFJzMizr7mfMdpr4uuUf8wMzUxMjAJFAwxUxuLexGLp48b6H0Ps4N6mvEov7xlAfXEIPiiSBXgAqSWzfZm0jtUlGIoRVzJCzSHnVOi7QSt_hA6PcZI-rlmzusVA&hsutk=&canon=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.biolinscientific.com%2Fblog%2Fcohesive-vs.-adhesive-failure-in-adhesive-bonding&ts=1773506484229 \"White paper  Adhesion - How to Use Contact Angle Measurements to Predict Adhesion and  Bonding? Home](https://www.ellsworth.com/) > [Resources](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/) > [Insights](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/insights/) > [Technical Bulletins](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/insights/) > Pros and Cons of Urethane, Epoxy, or Silicone Adhesives \n# Pros and Cons of Urethane, Epoxy, or Silicone Adhesives\n![Tom Steucek, ESR](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/tomsteucek.jpg)Tom Steucek  \n _Engineering Sales Representative_  \n _Ellsworth Adhesives_\nWith so many adhesive technologies available, choosing the right material for your application can be a daunting task. Epoxies, silicones, and urethanes are three primary chemistries widely used in industrial applications. Each has its own benefits and drawbacks to consider.\n![Epoxy](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/epoxy.jpg)\n#### Epoxy Adhesives\n[Epoxy adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/products/adhesives/epoxy/) are available in one or two-part formulations with room temperature and heat cure options. Known for strong bonds, they are a common structural-grade adhesive choice and are best at bonding similar substrates like glass, metal, and wood.\n**Pros of Using an Epoxy Adhesive**\n  * Good chemical resistance\n  * Excellent environmental resistance\n  * Tough, high-strength bonds\n  * Wide variety of cure speeds\n  * Good void-filling capabilities\n  * Can be electrically and thermally conductive\n  * Resistance to creep under sustained loads\n\n**Cons of Using an Epoxy Adhesive**\n  * Room temperature curing epoxies do not operate well over 120 \u00b0C, a heat cured epoxy is required\n  * Without modification, cured epoxies can be brittle\n  * Proper curing of two-part epoxies requires exact mix ratios\n  * Slow cure times\n\n![Silicone](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/silicone.jpg)\n#### Silicone Adhesives\n[Silicone adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/silicone.jpg) are available in one or two-part formulations and are widely known for being incredibly elastic and thermally stable. Their environmental resistance properties make them a great sealant choice against water and air penetration.\n**Pros of Using an Silicone Adhesive**\n  * Very flexible\n  * Excellent environmental resistance\n  * Room temperature curing silicones can easily operate over 150 \u00b0C\n  * Extremely low glass transition temperature (-100 \u00b0C or colder)\n  * Lower durometers and higher elongation\n  * Antimicrobial\n\n**Cons of Using an Silicone Adhesive**\n  * Poor adhesion to a large variety of substrates\n  * Poor chemical resistance\n  * Less resistance to aggressive fluids\n  * Low surface tension (cannot be painted)\n  * Cannot handle heavy loads\n  * Slow cure times\n\n![Urethane](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/urethane.jpg)\n#### Urethane Adhesives\n[Urethane adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/products/adhesives/urethane/), known for being durable and elastic, are available in moisture and heat cure systems, and work well in structural applications. They can bond both similar and dissimilar substrates including plastics, and typically don\u2019t require surface preparation.\n**Pros of Using an Urethane Adhesive**\n  * Lower durometers and higher elongation\n  * The \u201cgo to\u201d choice for submersion in water\n  * Better adhesion to a wide variety of plastics\n  * Excellent impact and vibration resistance\n  * Polybutadiene urethanes are used in underwater acoustic applications due to similar acoustic transmission to sea water\n  * Polybutadiene urethanes have low glass transition (Tg) temperatures, making them good for extreme cold electronic applications\n  * Low odor\n\n**Cons of Using an Urethane Adhesive**\n  * Does not operate well over 120 \u00b0C\n  * Less resistance to aggressive fluids\n  * Slow cure times\n  * Moisture sensitive prior to curing\n  * Difficult to integrate into an assembly process\n\n#### Creating a Custom Epoxy, Silicone, or Urethane Adhesive\nIn some instances, existing adhesives on the market don\u2019t have the right mix of chemical characteristics to meet unique application needs. For example, a company might be interested in the structural qualities of a particular epoxy adhesive, but their assembly process requires a faster cure time. Epoxy, silicone, and urethane adhesives can be modified or custom formulated to address these challenges. Other common reasons for developing a custom-formulated adhesive include:\n  * A material is slated for discontinuation, and your application requires a suitable replacement\n  * A material is no longer in regulatory compliance with REACH, RoHS, or Prop 65\n  * A material is a candidate for future non-compliance with REACH, RoHS, or Prop 65\n\n**![Resinlab-logo.jpg](https://www.ellsworth.com/globalassets/general/logos/resinlab-logo.jpg)**\n[ResinLab](https://www.ellsworth.com/manufacturers/resinlab/), a sister company of Ellsworth Adhesives, has a [full-service laboratory](https://www.resinlab.com/services/lab-services/) and team of chemists who use a variety of equipment and testing methods to create a custom epoxy, silicone, or urethane. Common services performed during new product development include:\n  * Rheological analysis: viscosity, cure response, and yield stress.\n  * Thermal analysis: coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE), glass transition temperature (Tg), softening (HDT), melting temperature, compression modulus, specific heat (Cp), thermal diffusivity, etc.\n  * Electrical analysis: electrical conductivity, dielectric strength, bulk/volume resistivity, and surface resistivity.\n  * Chemical analysis: chemical composition, impurity analysis, and chemical comparison.\n  * Mechanical analysis: adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress, tear strength, flexural strength, elongation, and modulus.\n\nFor many manufacturers, investing in a custom material provides long-term benefits for both the application and the bottom line.\n#### Ask the Glue Doctor\u00ae\nMaking the right material choice is crucial to ensuring your application is successful, with variables like cost, substrates, and assembly environment coming into play. Ellsworth Adhesives [Glue Doctors\u00ae](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/ask-the-glue-doctor/) are uniquely equipped with decades of application and product selection experience to help make the process simple. Connect with the team to find the right adhesive option for your unique application:\n[Download Technical Bulletin](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/159fc2d5e99a4a2a86c36d7571018ad0/ellsworth-adhesives-technical-bulletin---pros-and-cons-of-urethane-epoxy-or-silicone-adhesives.pdf)\n### Contact Home](https://www.ellsworth.com/) > [Resources](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/) > [Insights](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/insights/) > [Technical Bulletins](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/insights/) > Pros and Cons of Urethane, Epoxy, or Silicone Adhesives \n# Pros and Cons of Urethane, Epoxy, or Silicone Adhesives\n![Tom Steucek, ESR](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/tomsteucek.jpg)Tom Steucek  \n _Engineering Sales Representative_  \n _Ellsworth Adhesives_\nWith so many adhesive technologies available, choosing the right material for your application can be a daunting task. Epoxies, silicones, and urethanes are three primary chemistries widely used in industrial applications. Each has its own benefits and drawbacks to consider.\n![Epoxy](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/epoxy.jpg)\n#### Epoxy Adhesives\n[Epoxy adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/products/adhesives/epoxy/) are available in one or two-part formulations with room temperature and heat cure options. Known for strong bonds, they are a common structural-grade adhesive choice and are best at bonding similar substrates like glass, metal, and wood.\n**Pros of Using an Epoxy Adhesive**\n  * Good chemical resistance\n  * Excellent environmental resistance\n  * Tough, high-strength bonds\n  * Wide variety of cure speeds\n  * Good void-filling capabilities\n  * Can be electrically and thermally conductive\n  * Resistance to creep under sustained loads\n\n**Cons of Using an Epoxy Adhesive**\n  * Room temperature curing epoxies do not operate well over 120 \u00b0C, a heat cured epoxy is required\n  * Without modification, cured epoxies can be brittle\n  * Proper curing of two-part epoxies requires exact mix ratios\n  * Slow cure times\n\n![Silicone](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/silicone.jpg)\n#### Silicone Adhesives\n[Silicone adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/silicone.jpg) are available in one or two-part formulations and are widely known for being incredibly elastic and thermally stable. Their environmental resistance properties make them a great sealant choice against water and air penetration.\n**Pros of Using an Silicone Adhesive**\n  * Very flexible\n  * Excellent environmental resistance\n  * Room temperature curing silicones can easily operate over 150 \u00b0C\n  * Extremely low glass transition temperature (-100 \u00b0C or colder)\n  * Lower durometers and higher elongation\n  * Antimicrobial\n\n**Cons of Using an Silicone Adhesive**\n  * Poor adhesion to a large variety of substrates\n  * Poor chemical resistance\n  * Less resistance to aggressive fluids\n  * Low surface tension (cannot be painted)\n  * Cannot handle heavy loads\n  * Slow cure times\n\n![Urethane](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/836632cca8f04cbe94341a7d11056a47/urethane.jpg)\n#### Urethane Adhesives\n[Urethane adhesives](https://www.ellsworth.com/products/adhesives/urethane/), known for being durable and elastic, are available in moisture and heat cure systems, and work well in structural applications. They can bond both similar and dissimilar substrates including plastics, and typically don\u2019t require surface preparation.\n**Pros of Using an Urethane Adhesive**\n  * Lower durometers and higher elongation\n  * The \u201cgo to\u201d choice for submersion in water\n  * Better adhesion to a wide variety of plastics\n  * Excellent impact and vibration resistance\n  * Polybutadiene urethanes are used in underwater acoustic applications due to similar acoustic transmission to sea water\n  * Polybutadiene urethanes have low glass transition (Tg) temperatures, making them good for extreme cold electronic applications\n  * Low odor\n\n**Cons of Using an Urethane Adhesive**\n  * Does not operate well over 120 \u00b0C\n  * Less resistance to aggressive fluids\n  * Slow cure times\n  * Moisture sensitive prior to curing\n  * Difficult to integrate into an assembly process\n\n#### Creating a Custom Epoxy, Silicone, or Urethane Adhesive\nIn some instances, existing adhesives on the market don\u2019t have the right mix of chemical characteristics to meet unique application needs. For example, a company might be interested in the structural qualities of a particular epoxy adhesive, but their assembly process requires a faster cure time. Epoxy, silicone, and urethane adhesives can be modified or custom formulated to address these challenges. Other common reasons for developing a custom-formulated adhesive include:\n  * A material is slated for discontinuation, and your application requires a suitable replacement\n  * A material is no longer in regulatory compliance with REACH, RoHS, or Prop 65\n  * A material is a candidate for future non-compliance with REACH, RoHS, or Prop 65\n\n**![Resinlab-logo.jpg](https://www.ellsworth.com/globalassets/general/logos/resinlab-logo.jpg)**\n[ResinLab](https://www.ellsworth.com/manufacturers/resinlab/), a sister company of Ellsworth Adhesives, has a [full-service laboratory](https://www.resinlab.com/services/lab-services/) and team of chemists who use a variety of equipment and testing methods to create a custom epoxy, silicone, or urethane. Common services performed during new product development include:\n  * Rheological analysis: viscosity, cure response, and yield stress.\n  * Thermal analysis: coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE), glass transition temperature (Tg), softening (HDT), melting temperature, compression modulus, specific heat (Cp), thermal diffusivity, etc.\n  * Electrical analysis: electrical conductivity, dielectric strength, bulk/volume resistivity, and surface resistivity.\n  * Chemical analysis: chemical composition, impurity analysis, and chemical comparison.\n  * Mechanical analysis: adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress, tear strength, flexural strength, elongation, and modulus.\n\nFor many manufacturers, investing in a custom material provides long-term benefits for both the application and the bottom line.\n#### Ask the Glue Doctor\u00ae\nMaking the right material choice is crucial to ensuring your application is successful, with variables like cost, substrates, and assembly environment coming into play. Ellsworth Adhesives [Glue Doctors\u00ae](https://www.ellsworth.com/resources/ask-the-glue-doctor/) are uniquely equipped with decades of application and product selection experience to help make the process simple. Connect with the team to find the right adhesive option for your unique application:\n[Download Technical Bulletin](https://www.ellsworth.com/contentassets/159fc2d5e99a4a2a86c36d7571018ad0/ellsworth-adhesives-technical-bulletin---pros-and-cons-of-urethane-epoxy-or-silicone-adhesives.pdf)\n### Contact .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5257:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353.json:4469:      "content": "Title: Pull-Off Adhesion Test for Coatings on Large Pipes: possible variations in failure location and mode URL Source: https://www.nwpipe.com/app/uploads/2020/08/Adhesion-Testing-Croll.pdf Published Time: Wed, 26 Feb 2025 15:14:35 GMT Number of Pages: 15 Markdown Content: Pull-Off Adhesion Test for Coatings on Large Pipes: possible variations in failure location and mode. S. G. Croll 1 , C. Siripirom 1 , B. D. Keil 21 North Dakota State University, Dept. of Coatings and Polymeric Materials, P.O. Box 6050, Dept 2760, Fargo, ND 58108-6050. PH (701) 231-9415. E-mail: Stuart.Croll@ndsu.edu 2 Northwest Pipe Company, 5721 SE Columbia Way, Suite 200, Vancouver, WA 98661. PH (360) 397-6343. E-mail: bkeil@nwpipe.com Abstract Pull-off adhesion testing per ASTM D-4541 is a commonly used quality control check for coatings on large diameter steel water pipe. During the testing a metal dolly is glued to a pipeline coating then pulled off, to assess adhesion of the coating. In practice, results are very sensitive to circumstances and have large standard deviations compared to their mean value. It is clear from a simple inspection of Griffith\u2019s equation for the strength of materials that the pull-off stress at failure increases with the stiffness of the materials, the interfacial failure energy and is diminished by presence of existing cracks or flaws. Finite element stress analysis explored the effect of the coating and glue stiffness as well as some of the other possible reasons for variation in the results, i.e. pipe curvature, misalignment of the dolly, as well as the effect of scoring through the coating and glue around the dolly. The tensile modulus of three polyurethane coatings, an epoxy adhesive and a cyanoacrylate adhesive were measured and showed that it was possible for a polyurethane coating to be stiffer than an adhesive used to fix the dolly in place. The location of the maximum strain, in the glue or the coating, was used as an indicator of where the adhesive failure was likely to occur within the overall joint. Results indicate that the influence of dolly misalignment on the coated pipe is greater than the influence of the pipe curvature itself. If the dolly was aligned perfectly, the greatest strain, and thus most likely location of failure was on the crest of the pipe at the pipe-coating interface. The value of the strain was not a strong function of the curvature of the underlying pipe surface. If the dolly was misaligned, then the locus of failure shifted to where the glue was thinnest, but remained at the coating-pipe interface, if the glue is stiffer than the coating. However, if the coating was stiffer than the glue, then the location of the > 1319 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 greatest strain indicated that failure was more likely to be at the glue-dolly junction in all cases, rather than at the coating-pipe interface. Users should be aware of how these, and other possible, variations affect the pull-off results if they rely upon single dolly pull values to assess the overall adhesion or use the test method to assess the likely reliability of a coated steel pipe in service. Introduction Steel pipelines used to transport water often have large fractions of their length buried underground, or otherwise inaccessible. One of the principal threats to the long term life of the pipeline is corrosion of the steel, so external polyurethane coatings are applied to prevent water and dissolved salts from attacking the pipes. Pull-off adhesion testing of the coating to the pipe, according to ASTM D-4541, is a universally used procedure to check the quality of the coating and its application to a properly prepared steel pipe. If the values obtained meet a standard, for example AWWA C222, then depending on the results of other tests, the coated pipe is deemed suitable for long term service. The pull-off adhesion test is notorious for the variation in the results [Devries 2002, Ramos 2012, Croll 2012] and this paper explores some of the reasons for the variation. In practice, the test is performed just after the pipe sections are coated, in the factory, or in storage, or at the job site. Thus the test is performed under environmental conditions that vary from cold to hot, humid or dry, and so on. Commonly, the test is performed where the engineer can reach, which might be on the side of a large diameter pipe. One can already appreciate the difficulty in obtaining reproducible results. Causes of variability include problems in surface preparation, adhesive application, curing, thickness and evenness of adhesive, alignment of the pull-off dolly and variation introduced by the operator of the test equipment. The investigation here focuses on two forms of misalignment; when the substrate is not flat, as is specified in ASTM D-4541, and when the dolly is not set parallel to the substrate. The need to maintain perfect alignment in the pull-off test has been realized for many years [Anderson 1 1988] where it is suggested that good techniques can reduce the standard deviation to 10% of the mean, or less. This has also been suggested in other publications dealing with detailed stress analysis of adhesive joints [Anderson 2 1988]. But, clearly, normal practice makes this difficult to achieve. Large differences in values are seen, even using the same materials, but at different institutions [Ikegami, 1996][ASTM 2009]. There are two complementary approaches to understanding the strength of a material or an adhesive joint. The energy approach, like other thermodynamic approaches, indicates whether a change, here the adhesive failure, may occur. This is often the > 1320 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 simplest approach and may be all that is necessary. Stress analysis is much more complicated, but may be necessary because it provides the mechanistic information, indicating where the failure is most likely to start and progress. Analyses of material systems, including adhesive joints, assume that the adhesive materials are homogenous and continuous. Typical coatings contain pigment particles, extender particles, may be phase separated and exhibit other inhomogeneities. In most discussions of adhesion, a coating or adhesive is assumed to be a single, deformable homogenous layer between two rigid adherends, and that the average stress across the area of the joint is sufficient to characterize the strength of the joint. In testing adhesion of coatings, the glue used to adhere the dolly to the coating is usually assumed to be part of the dolly and not to require consideration in the analysis. This implies that it must be very rigid compared to the coating, as well as adhere very well. Except in the most detailed analyses, the materials that form the joint are assumed to obey Hooke\u2019s law, i.e. be linear and elastic in their mechanical properties. In contrast, polymeric coatings and adhesives are intrinsically non-linear, viscoelastic materials. Thermodynamic Approach to Adhesion Failure Griffith\u2019s equation [Griffith 1921] for the strength of materials was a very important advance since it recognized that most material\u2019s strength was limited by cracks and flaws that were already present. The equation describes how brittle failure depends on the properties of materials and that failure is due to enlargement of a crack or flaw. A crack in this context may be actual damage or an interface where the adhesion between polymer and a pigment particle, for example, is very weak. 2 External Built in E Stress Stress a ## \u03b3\u03c3 \u03c0 \u2212= = + Where: E = Young\u2019s modulus of the material \u03b3 = Interfacial energy of adhesion/area = energy required to create new crack surface within a material or interface (strain energy release rate) a = radius of the existing crack (assuming a circular shape) \u03c3 = Griffith fracture stress, the critical stress above which brittle failure occurs as a crack propagates, this is usually applied externally, but if the materials already have stress built in, from their curing chemistry, for example, the amount of external stress required is less. The tensile modulus is appropriate in this equation because failure usually opens the crack wider, and stretches the material at the crack tip. This equation is often applied to polymer failure because failure usually occurs, suddenly, at very high strain-rates across the crack tip, and polymers are approximately brittle under these circumstances. If the polymer does not behave like a brittle, linear elastic material, then \u03b3 must include the energy dissipated in processes leading up to the crack propagating, e.g. > 1321 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 yielding, fibril formation etc. which can give energies orders of magnitude higher than that for crack propagation only [Shull 2000, Creton 2000, Crosby 2000]. The Griffith equation has been used widely in engineering and physical science, to describe the failure of many materials. When one measures the stress necessary to pull a dolly and the adhering coating away from the substrate, it gives a value for the term on the right hand side of the equation. It does not give a value for \u03b3 which is the parameter relevant for the adhesion and there is no way to separate this parameter without other information. Any internal stress, caused by curing shrinkage or accidents of coating or glue application will diminish the external stress necessary for fracture. Pull-off values can be increased by coatings that have higher values of modulus, have smaller pre-existing cracks and have other mechanisms for dissipating energy within the materials. Adding a filler to a polymer increases the mechanical modulus and may increase the measured pull-off, as pointed out above. However, fillers may agglomerate and diminish the strength considerably because the rupture will be initiated at the agglomerate, i.e. they produced a large Griffith flaw. Large (micro) particles of filler have stress concentrations around them that form at the poles (even if the particle was perfectly spherical without sharp edges) with respect to the stress direction [Fond 2001] so the interface around a filler is a likely starting point for rupture. There is considerable interest in the use of nano-fillers where the particle size is less than the yield zone of the polymer and so one should get the advantage of the increase in modulus without the disadvantage of causing a large flaw that initiates failure. Unfortunately, nano-particles are very difficult to disperse so they can form agglomerates that are much larger and so form larger flaws and so lose the advantage. The pull-off test for adhesion often produces a failure within the coating or within the glue, or at an interface or failure that changes its location across the overall area of the fracture. In a typical test, we have the coating and glue that may fail in a cohesive sense and we have the coating-substrate, coating-glue, glue-dolly interfaces that may fail in an adhesive sense. The failure will occur at those locations, adhesive or cohesive, that due to their properties offer the least resistance to the external stress. There is a variant by Kendall [Kendall 1971] of the Griffith approach specifically for an adhesive between two perfectly rigid adherends, that ignores the possibility of pre-existing cracks and cohesive failure. It gives a result in a similar form: Kt ## \u03b3 ## \u03c3 = Where: \u03c3 = pull-off adhesion stress > 1322 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 K = bulk modulus of the adhesive layer since it is constrained between rigid bodies and no crack exists \u03b3 = interfacial energy of adhesion/area t = thickness of the adhesive/coating layer Again, the measured pull-off stress is a value determined by a combination of parameters, not solely the interfacial adhesion. The interfacial energy of adhesion must depend on the nature and number of the interactions between the coating material and the material of the substrate. Coatings studied by this test are rarely chemically linked to the substrate, nor interpenetrate the substrate. The interactions between a polymer and a substrate will depend on the polymer chain conformation at the substrate and the polymer moieties that are interacting with the material of the substrate. Polymer modulus will depend on the bulk properties of the composition, including crosslink density, chain stiffness etc. Interfacial interactions are not necessarily dictated by the bulk properties of the coating. Even if the alignment in the pull-off test is perfect and all the other conditions have been met rigorously, the stress distribution along the bond line is not even. It is well known that stress concentrations occur at the edges of joints, at crack tips or other heterogeneities. The path of the failure crack can be understood from the stress intensities and the properties of the materials at the microscopic level. The expenditure of mechanical energy in all the processes leading up to failure will depend, of course, on these stress concentrations and their locations. Average stress across the whole joint area is significantly smaller than that at the stress concentration and since the failure will be determined by the maximum stress, average stress is not useful for correlation with other properties. The energy approach is simple, but it has already shown that in the dolly-glue-coating-substrate system, the pull-off test might produce a variety of results depending on the interfacial or cohesive fracture energies, material stiffnesses, and which material or interface has a crucial flaw. In order to gain more insight about how material properties and test geometry affect the how the adhesive or cohesive failure occurs, it is necessary to examine the stress, or strain, distribution within the system. Stress Distribution Films between a rigid dolly and a rigid substrate, are constrained by adhesion on both sides. Thus there can be a large hydrostatic stress due to this confinement which is largest in the center. This effect will be greater in less compressible materials, with a Poisson\u2019s ratio closer to 0.5, see Figure 1. In practice, the coating thickness is much less than in the cartoon here, so the effect is greater. In reality the coating (and the glue) is not cut perfectly even with the dolly circumference, but extends, somewhat inconsistently, little way beyond. > 1323 Pipelines 2014: From Underground to the Forefront of Innovation and Sustainability \u00a9 ASCE 2014 Coating/adhesive Substrate > Pulling off force Lateral strain > Stresses anywhere in coating are 3-D > Dolly Figure 1. Schematic of the pull-off adhesion test illustrating how constraints produced by adhering to a rigid substrate and a rigid dolly cause multi-directional strains in the deformable coatin"

Title and Subtitle  5. Report Date \n\nEvaluation of Different Paint Systems for Over -Coating \n\nExisting Structural Steel \n\nAugust 2018 \n\n> 6. Performing Organization Code\n\n7. Author(s)  8. Perform ing Organization Report No. \n\nBalaguru, Perumalsamy; Najm, Husam and David Caronia  \n\n> 9. Performing Organization Name and Address 10. Contract or Grant No.\n> 12. Sponsoring Agency Name and Address 13. Type of Report and Period Covered\n> Federal Highway Adminis tration\n> 1200 New Jersey Av, SE\n> U.S. Department of Transportation\n> Washington, D.C. 20590\n\nFinal Report \n\n> 14. Sponsoring Agency Code\n> 15. Supplementary Notes\n> 16. Abstract\n\nThe project was undertaken to identify a procedure t o accept new coating systems for over -coating of steel \n\nsurfaces. The practices used by various state transportation agencies, and test methods for accelerated testing of \n\ncoatings with primary focus on ASTM and FHWA guidelines were reviewed. It was conclude d that a new \n\nprotocol for accepting new systems that will provide results in a timely manner is required. Therefore, a new \n\nprotocol for evaluating durability of coatings and their effectiveness in reducing corrosion of steel structures \n\nwas developed. Resul ts presented in this report focuses on the details of the new protocol, recommendations for \n\nacceptance and a plan for implementation. Basic parameters of relevant ASTM specifications and guidelines \n\nprovided in FHWA publications are incorporated in the test method. The major difference between the \n\nproposed method and the current practice is the way the corrosion creep from a coating -damaged location is \n\nmeasured after exposure to accelerated corrosive conditions. The proposed method is based on direct pull -off \n\n(adhesion) strength at various stages of corrosion. These pull -off strengths provide quantitative and repeatable \n\nmeasurements for quantifying the degradation. In the area of accelerated exposure conditions, deep freezing is \n\nincorporated as part of the accelerated degradation process. These two measures provide significant and clearly \n\nmeasurable degradation within three months of accelerated exposure. Coatings that are known to provide \n\nexcellent and weak corrosion protection were tested using the propos ed protocol and the results show a clear \n\ndifference between the best and the poor coatings. The test resu lts also correlate well with results of the long -\n\nterm field study. As expected, corrosion creep from a damaged -coating location is the primary contribu tor to \n\ndegradation of coatings. Other degradation indicators such thinning, color change, influence of welding and \n\nbolt hole locations were also evaluated. Acceptance criteria for these degradation mechanisms are also \n\nincorporated in the acceptance criteri a. In the area of quality assurance, this study evaluates correlation \n\ncoefficients for structural steel paints by performing Infra -Red (IR) scan us ing ASTM specifications E 2937 .\n\nChemical signatures have established for the components of six coating syste ms that are currently approved for \n\nuse by New Jersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT). \n\n17. Key Words  18. Distribution Statement \n\nOver -coating, accelerated testing, steel surfaces, \n\ncorrosion growth, field performance   \n\n> 19. Security Classif (of this re port) 20. The practices used by various state transportation \n\nagencies, and test methods for accelerated testing of coatings with primary focus on \n\nAmerican Society for Testing Materials ( ASTM ) and Federal Highway Administration \n\n(FHWA ) guidelines were reviewed. It was concluded that a new protocol for accepting \n\nnew systems is needed. This new protocol should provide results in a timely manner ,\n\npreferably within nine months and it should be reasonably economical to conduct the \n\ntest s. Therefore, a new protocol for evaluating durability of coatings and their \n\neffectiveness in reducing corrosion of steel structures was developed. Results \n\npresented in this report focuses on the details of the new protocol, recommendations for \n\nacceptance criteria and a plan for implementation. Basic parameters of relevant ASTM \n\nspecifications and guidelines provided in FHWA publications are incorporated in the test \n\nmethod. The major difference between the proposed method and the current practice is \n\nthe way the corrosion creep from a coating -damaged location is measured after \n\nexposure to accelerated corrosive conditions. The proposed method is based on direct \n\npull -off (adhesion) strength at various stages of corrosion. These pull -off strengths \n\nprovide quanti tative and repeatable measurements for quantifying the degradation. In \n\nthe area of accelerated exposure conditions, deep freezing is incorporated as part of the \n\naccelerated degradation process. These two measures provide significant and clearly \n\nmeasurable degradation within three months of accelerated exposure. Coatings that are \n\nknown to provide excellent and weak corrosion protection were tested using the \n\nproposed protocol and the results show a clear difference between the best and the \n\npoor coatings. The test results also correlate well with one of the long -term field study. \n\nAs expected, corrosion creep from a damaged -coating location is the primary \n\ncontributor to degradation of coatings. Other degradation indicators such as thinning, \n\ncolor change, influe nce of welding and bolt hole locations were als o evaluated. \n\nAcceptance limits for these degradation mechanisms are also incorporated in the \n\nacceptance criteria. A methodology for quality assurance of the accepted products \n\nduring their use is also presented . This methodology is based on the current New \n\nJersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT) procedure used for coatings and \n\nadmixtures. The following are the key conclusions and recommendations :\n\n\uf0b7 The effectiveness and acceptability of new coating system can be determined \n\nwithin six months. \n\n\uf0b7 The new test protocol provides clear quantitatively measurable results for \n\nevaluating corrosion vulnerability. \n\n\uf0b7 The results obtained using accelerated corrosion exposure in the laboratory \n\ncorrelate well with the 20 -year fiel d-study results conducted by NJDOT (Mathis \n\nBridge). \n\n\uf0b7 Creeping of corrosion from a damaged or weak coating location is the primary \n\ncontributing factor for degradation of coating systems. \n\n\uf0b7 Among the coating systems currently available in the market , those con taining an \n\ninorganic zinc or organic zinc primer provides the best performance .\n\n\uf0b7 The epoxy systems and aluminum -mastic systems performed wor st 2\n\nIt is recommended to implement the new protocol for accepting any new coatings and at \n\nth e same time seek support from FHWA and other state DOTs for wider application of \n\nthe protocol. An efficient way to attain the acceptance by other states is to conduct a \n\nbroader study with multi -state and laboratories participation. This could be achieved \n\nusing a pool -fund study. A plan for the implementation is also included in this report. \n\nIn the area of quality assurance, this study evaluates correlation coefficients for \n\nstructural steel paints by performing IR scan using ASTM specifications C494 -05a. Th e\n\nintent of this study is to perform a sufficient number of scans from different batches of \n\nthe same sample as supplied by the manufacturer to establish baseline correlation \n\nvalues of individual steel paints. These scans are then analyzed using statistical \n\nmethods and the correlation coefficients for the steel paint systems were established. \n\nThese correlation values will be used as quantitative measures to interpret, accept or \n\nreject job samples. A total of six structural steel paint components used by NJDO T were \n\ntested. They include primary coats, secondary coats, thin films, and others. The results \n\nof this study include methodology, test procedures, scan data, and correlation \n\ncoefficients for quantitative assessment of the most commonly used structural ste el \n\npaints on the qualified p roducer/supplier QPL NJDOT list 3\n\nBACKGROUND \n\nA common practice for maintaining and repairing existing structural steel is to apply an \n\nepoxy mastic urethane overcoating. The Society for Protective Coatings (SSPC) defines \n\nthis prac tice as follows: \n\nOvercoating is defined as the application of coating materials over an existing \n\ncoating in order to extend its service life, including use of the appropriate \n\ncleaning methods. The procedure includes preparation of rusted or degraded \n\nareas, feathering edges of existing paint, low -pressure water washing of the \n\nentire structure to remove contaminants, application of a full intermediate coat \n\nover repaired areas, and optional application of a full topcoat over the entire \n\nstructure (41 ).\n\nPrior t o 2016 NJDOT approved new systems for overcoating based on the evaluation \n\nand recommendation of NEPCOAT . This organization discontinued their test program. \n\nThis project was undertaken to identify acceptance criteria for new over -coating \n\nsystems. If practic es currently used by other agencies including various department of \n\ntransportation are not satisfactory, a new test protocol was to be developed for use by \n\nNew Jersey Department of Transportation ( NJDOT ). The decision to develop a new test \n\nprocedure was ma de by NJDOT panel after the research team presented the current \n\nstate of the art and the need for a new method. The primary weakness es of the current \n\npractice are the time and effort needed to evaluate new coating systems and the \n\ndifficulty in measuring th e outcome after exposure to accelerated corrosion. If a new test \n\nmethod is proposed, it had to be validated and incorporated in the proposed new \n\nacceptance criteria. In addition, a plan was to be presented for implementation of the \n\nfindings. \n\nInfrared (IR) spectroscopy was identified as an effective tool for quality assurance .\n\nThe IR spectroscopy is typically used for the following cases: 1) the sample (or \n\nspectrum) is a \u2018\u2018total unknown\u2019\u2019 and an identification is required \u2013 examples include \n\nforensic samples , environmental waste samples, or new discovery samples, where a \n\nnew material has been synthesized or discovered, 2) the sample (or spectrum) is an \n\nunknown and it needs to be characterized or classified \u2013 examples include commercial \n\napplications where new additives or components are included in a material to provide a \n\nspecific property; in such cases this could be considered the basis of competitive \n\nproduct analysis, 3) the sample generally is known but the existence of a specific \n\nchemical class needs to be determined \u2013examples include contaminant analysis, \n\nanalysis for toxicology or environmental reasons, material additives, etc., and 4) the \n\nsample is a complete known and the interpretation is required to confirm the material \n\ncomposition and/or quality \u2013 ex amples include product quality control and the \n\nconfirmation of a structure or functionality of a newly synthesized material. \n\nThe work in this project focused on scenario 4, where the samples are known, and \n\nquantitative assessment and quality control were r equired. Chemical signatures of all \n\nthe components for 6 currently used systems were obtained for checking future \n\nsupplies .4\n\nOBJECTIVES \n\nThe primary objective was to develop a protocol for accepting new systems for over -\n\ncoating. NJDOT discontinued using NEP COAT recommendations for accepting new \n\ncoatings in 2016 . Therefore, an efficient and fair protocol was needed for accepting new \n\ncoating systems in the list of \u201cApproved Coatings\u201d. This primary objective can be \n\ndivided into following secondary objectives :\n\n\uf0b7 Review practices used by other states and other agencies to prepare a summary \n\nof current practices .\n\n\uf0b7 Recommend a protocol , that is based on the current practices used by other \n\nstate DOTs or agencies, for accepting new coating systems to NJDOT panel . If \n\nnone of the current practice is acceptable, propose a new protocol/test method \n\noutlining the strengths of the proposed test and obtain approval for evaluating the \n\nproposed test .\n\n\uf0b7 Prepare details of the test program and obtain suggestions/modifications from \n\nthe N JDOT Panel .\n\n\uf0b7 Evaluate the proposed test method using four to six currently used coating \n\nsystems .\n\n\uf0b7 Present the results obtained using the proposed test method to the Panel with \n\nrecommendations and if the test is successful, prepare the acceptance criteria. \n\n\uf0b7 Pr epare an implementation plan .\n\n\uf0b7 Provide meaningful interpretations of the IR Scans of structural steel paints and \n\nestablish correlations for IR scans of manufacturer samples .\n\n\uf0b7 Establish acceptability criteria and determine acceptable tolerances for job \n\nsamp les compared to manufacturer acceptance samples on the NJDOT qualified \n\nproducer/supplier (QPL) list. \n\nThese objectives were to be achieved in two phases. In Phase 1 , the primar y focus was \n\non Literature Review resulting in a summary and recommendation to ad opt an existing \n\nprotocol or propose a new protocol . If the NJDOT Panel approves, conduct Phase 2 that \n\nwill focus on evaluating the proposed test method, developing an acceptance criterion \n\nand preparing an implementation plan. \n\nPhase I \u2013 Literature Review \n\nPhase II \u2013 Research Work plan \n\nTask 1 - Selection of potential test methods to be used for acceptance criteria \n\nTask 2 - Evaluation of potential test methods for their suitability for inclusion in \n\nacceptance criteria \n\nTask 3 - Development of protocols for accepting new products \n\nTask 4 - Develop guidelines for surface preparation and coating application \n\nTask 5 - Prepare IR Scans for six systems that are currently used \n\nTask 6 - Prepare an i mplementation plan 5\n\nINTRODUCTION \n\nA common practice for maintaining and repairing existing structural steel is to apply an \n\nepox y mastic urethane overcoating. NJDOT has an active over -coating program, a \n\ntypical application example is shown in Figure 1. \n\nFigure 1 . An Over -coated beam:  US Route 202 over NJ Route 29 before  and \n\nafter Over -coating \n\nOvercoating offers significant adva ntages over repainting a structural steel bridge. The \n\nprincipal advantage is cost. According to a n FHWA article (21) , full removal of paint can \n\ncost as much as $35 per square foot, because the old paint typically includes lead, \n\nwhich is a hazardous materia l to humans and the environment. In contrast, overcoat \n\napplications can cost an agency "

failure](https://www.biolinscientific.com/hs-fs/hubfs/adhesive%20vs.%20cohesive%20failure.jpg?height=480&name=adhesive%20vs.%20cohesive%20failure.jpg)\n[Susanna Laur\u00e9n](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/author/susanna-laur%C3%A9n) Sep 14, \u201921 > 3 min \n# Cohesive vs. adhesive failure in adhesive bonding\n##  [ ](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog)\nAdhesive bonding is a process where two similar or dissimilar materials are fastened together. As the name implies, in adhesive bonding, an adhesive, typically some kind of glue is used to bond the two surfaces. In the adhesive bonding process, both adhesive and cohesive forces are involved. Depending on the strength of these forces, the adhesion failure can be either adhesive, cohesive, or substrate failure. \n## Adhesive and cohesive forces are involved in the bonding process\nWhen the glue is applied on the surface, its ability to [wet the surface](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/wettability-determines-the-adhesion-of-coatings) is of utmost importance for good adhesion. Good wettability ensures that the glue can penetrate surface structures which leads to better [mechanical interlocking](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/what-is-adhesion) at the interface. The adhesive forces between the glue and the substrate as well as the cohesive forces in the glue determine how well the glue can wet the surface. If the cohesive forces inside the glue (or any other coating formulation for that matter) are high, the glue prefers to stay together rather than interact with the surface. However, if the adhesive interaction between the substrate and the glue is high enough, the glue might spread after all. Once the glue has hardened and the interfaces are formed, these same forces determine whether the bonding is permanent or adhesion failure is likely to happen.\n## Adhesion failure mechanisms\nTo understand why bonding fails, we need to first define the ways the failure can occur. ![Bonding failure_light](https://www.biolinscientific.com/hs-fs/hubfs/images/Biolin%20Scientific/Blog/Bonding%20failure_light.jpg?width=1508&name=Bonding%20failure_light.jpg)These can be divided into three categories:\n  * Adhesive failure \n  * Cohesive failure\n  * Substrate failure \n\nAdhesive failure, or delamination, is one of the most common types of failure mechanisms. There the two dissimilar materials detach from each other. The failure can happen between the adhesive and either of the two substrates, it is bonding together. Cohesive failure happens in the adhesive itself or inside the layer of a coating. Substrate failure is not related to the bonding process itself as it is a problem in a substrate.\nAs you might guess already, the cohesive failure will happen if the cohesive forces inside the adhesive are not strong enough. Then, on the other hand, the adhesive failure happens if the glue has not spread properly on the substrate. To make sure that the spreading of the glue (or other coating formulation) is sufficient, contact angle measurements can be used.\nTo read more about adhesion and how contact angle measurements can help to predict its success, please download the white paper through the link below.\n## [White paper Adhesion - How to Use Contact Angle Measurements to Predict Adhesion and Bonding? Download](https://www.biolinscientific.com/cs/c/?cta_guid=9e9bf7f1-5bc8-4f1c-af24-566e15e1cf68&signature=AAH58kFLTiz9RY7fwCL0SnjhkNOn8idLSw&portal_id=516902&pageId=55198155677&placement_guid=a852e4a4-d28d-4f47-bf75-b5340e0be0e7&click=e4bd2eca-f7b6-4427-b7ee-cc622b5fd633&redirect_url=APefjpHlxVmk_vF_eDTG_LgWBDaQ6vroCNVT75qMFJzMizr7mfMdpr4uuUf8wMzUxMjAJFAwxUxuLexGLp48b6H0Ps4N6mvEov7xlAfXEIPiiSBXgAqSWzfZm0jtUlGIoRVzJCzSHnVOi7QSt_hA6PcZI-rlmzusVA&hsutk=&canon=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.biolinscientific.com%2Fblog%2Fcohesive-vs.-adhesive-failure-in-adhesive-bonding&ts=1773506484229 \"White paper  Adhesion - How to Use Contact Angle Measurements to Predict Adhesion and  Bonding? (4)](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/author/jyrki-korpela)\n  * [Anna Oom (4)](https://www.biolinscientific.com/blog/author/anna-oom)\n  * [Michael Rodal, PhD
